proposals.title: Non-Instantaneous and Non-Degenerate Two-Photon Absorption Dynamics in Graded-Index and Space-Time Varying Media
proposals.motivation: The standard model for two-photon absorption (TPA) assumes an instantaneous, degenerate response, which is inadequate for accurately modeling ultrafast pulse propagation in advanced photonic media. Pre-2026 work on space-time varying media and graded-index (GRIN) fibers has revealed complex pulse trajectories and reflection phenomena where finite material response times and frequency-dependent absorption could play a critical role. A more precise model is needed to predict nonlinear losses and pulse shaping in next-generation integrated photonic devices and ultrafast laser systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating a finite response time and non-degenerate photon energies into the TPA model will fundamentally alter the predicted nonlinear absorption and spectral broadening of ultrashort pulses, especially in media with engineered refractive index profiles or spatiotemporal variations.
proposals.method: We will develop a generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation (NLSE) incorporating a convolution-based TPA response function with a characteristic relaxation time, derived from a two-level system model. The non-degenerate case will be handled via a frequency-domain susceptibility. This model will be solved numerically using a split-step Fourier method, with boundary conditions informed by recent work on temporal reflection. We will simulate pulse propagation in GRIN fibers and designed space-time varying media, comparing results against the instantaneous degenerate model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous theoretical framework for TPA in the ultrafast regime, enabling accurate design of photonic devices that leverage nonlinear absorption for pulse control. It will directly extend the energy conservation relations for space-time media to include dispersive nonlinear losses, offering new insights into soliton deceleration and stability in the presence of realistic absorption.
proposals.novelty: While non-instantaneous Kerr response is well-studied, applying a similar treatment to the dissipative TPA process, particularly in the context of non-degenerate frequencies and engineered GRIN/space-time media, is a novel synthesis of concepts from nonlinear optics and ultrafast photonics.

proposals.title: Thermally Driven Self-Phase Modulation: The Role of Two-Photon Absorption Heating on the Nonlinear Refractive Index and Carrier Dynamics
proposals.motivation: In high-power, ultrafast nonlinear optics, self-phase modulation (SPM) is a cornerstone effect. However, its standard description assumes an instantaneous, temperature-independent Kerr coefficient (n2). In semiconductors and some glasses, intense pulses induce significant two-photon absorption (TPA), generating hot carriers whose recombination and thermalization locally heat the medium. This heating can dynamically alter n2 and the effective nonlinear response, a coupled thermo-optic effect not captured by conventional models, leading to discrepancies in spectral broadening predictions and device performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TPA-induced local heating creates a dynamic, temperature-dependent nonlinear refractive index, n2(T), which modifies the SPM-induced spectral broadening in a manner dependent on pulse energy, repetition rate, and the material's thermo-optic coefficient and carrier recombination lifetime.
proposals.method: We will develop a generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation coupled to a rate equation for carrier density and a heat diffusion equation. The model will incorporate a temperature-dependent n2(T) via the material's dn/dT, with the local temperature rise calculated from TPA-generated carrier recombination heat. We will implement a split-step Fourier method with a variational boundary condition framework to solve this system numerically. Experiments will use high-repetition-rate femtosecond pulses in semiconductor (e.g., silicon) and chalcogenide glass graded-index (GRIN) fibers, comparing measured SPM spectra with simulations from both standard and our new thermo-coupled model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles model linking TPA heating to dynamic nonlinear optical coefficients, quantitatively explaining deviations from classical SPM theory. It will yield a new generalized energy conservation relation for space-time varying media with thermal gradients, offering critical design guidelines for high-power integrated photonic devices and nonlinear pulse compressors where thermal management is paramount.
proposals.novelty: While thermal effects in lasers are well-known, the direct, dynamic coupling of TPA-generated heat to the instantaneous Kerr nonlinearity governing SPM is a novel and unexplored mechanism. This integrates Agrawal's recent work on temporal reflection in varying media and GRIN physics into the domain of thermo-optic nonlinear dynamics.

proposals.title: Temporal Coupled-Mode Theory for Resonant Stub-Waveguide Interactions in Graded-Index and Space-Time Varying Media
proposals.motivation: Resonant coupling between a waveguide and a side-coupled stub resonator is a fundamental building block for on-chip optical filtering, switching, and sensing. While extensively studied in static photonic crystals, a rigorous analytical framework for such interactions in complex media like graded-index (GRIN) fibers or space-time varying systems—relevant to pulse shaping and soliton dynamics—remains underdeveloped. This gap limits the design and understanding of dynamic, nonlinear, or dispersion-engineered resonant devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that temporal coupled-mode theory (TCMT), a powerful tool for photonic crystal cavities, can be formally adapted to describe stub-waveguide coupling in GRIN and space-time modulated media, providing closed-form expressions for resonant linewidth, coupling coefficients, and phase conditions that account for the medium's spatiotemporal variations.
proposals.method: We will derive the TCMT formalism by treating the stub as a localized resonator with a complex resonant frequency, coupled to the continuum of waveguide modes. The coupling coefficients and resonator decay rates will be expressed as overlap integrals of the unperturbed modal fields of the waveguide and stub, calculated using the known analytical solutions for parabolic GRIN media. For space-time varying systems, inspired by recent work on temporal reflections and generalized energy conservation, we will incorporate a time-dependent perturbation theory to model the dynamic coupling. The analytical model will be validated against full-wave FDTD simulations and experimental data from fabricated GRIN waveguide-stub structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified analytical model for resonant stub coupling beyond uniform media, directly applicable to pulse propagation in GRIN fibers and emerging space-time metamaterials. It will deliver explicit design rules for resonant linewidth engineering and dynamic tuning, advancing the design of integrated optical filters, delay lines, and nonlinear pulse processors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of TCMT—a staple of photonic crystal physics—with the physics of GRIN and space-time varying media, a core expertise of the researcher, to solve a classic waveguide problem in a new, dynamically controllable regime.

proposals.title: Large-Signal Frequency Chirp Dynamics in Optical Fibers with Coupled Gain-Index Nonlinearity: A Variational and Numerical Study
proposals.motivation: Frequency chirp induced by large-signal modulation critically limits the transmission bandwidth of directly modulated optical links, yet existing chirp models treat gain and refractive-index nonlinearities independently or rely on small-signal linearization. For next-generation short-reach coherent and intensity-modulated systems operating at high extinction ratios, an accurate large-signal chirp framework that couples the carrier-density-dependent gain with the intensity-dependent Kerr and carrier-induced index changes is essential. Developing such a model would enable improved dispersion management and signal-processing strategies in high-capacity optical networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a coupled nonlinear model incorporating both the carrier-density-dependent refractive index (linewidth-enhancement factor) and the Kerr-type intensity-dependent index simultaneously produces qualitatively distinct chirp transient profiles and spectral broadening regimes under large-signal modulation that cannot be captured by treating either nonlinearity in isolation.
proposals.method: We will derive a generalized coupled-mode framework that self-consistently links the semiconductor rate equations for carrier density and photon density with a nonlinear Schrodinger-type propagation equation containing both carrier-induced and Kerr index terms. A variational analysis using Gaussian-ansatz trial functions will be employed to extract semi-analytical expressions for instantaneous frequency chirp, pulse width evolution, and spectral broadening as functions of modulation depth and bias current. These variational predictions will be validated against split-step Fourier numerical simulations of the full coupled system across a range of modulation formats including non-return-to-zero and return-to-zero at 25 and 50 Gbaud. Parameter sweeps over the linewidth-enhancement factor (alpha = 2-7), Kerr coefficient, and modulation extinction ratio will map the boundaries between adiabatic-chirp-dominated and transient-chirp-dominated regimes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified semi-analytical description of large-signal chirp that accounts for the interplay between gain saturation, carrier-induced index change, and Kerr nonlinearity in a single variational framework. The resulting chirp maps and regime boundaries will serve as practical design tools for engineers optimizing directly modulated laser transmitters for short-reach and data-center interconnect applications. Additionally, the variational approach will offer physical insight into chirp compensation strategies that purely numerical methods obscure.
proposals.novelty: Prior variational treatments of pulse propagation in fibers by Agrawal and others have addressed Kerr and dispersive effects separately from semiconductor laser dynamics; coupling both index nonlinearities within a single variational ansatz under large-signal conditions is unexplored. The identification of distinct chirp regimes arising from this coupling represents a non-obvious extension beyond the standard alpha-parameter formalism.

proposals.title: Bifurcation Analysis and Stability Mapping of Generalized Maxwell-Bloch Systems in Graded-Index and Space-Time Varying Media
proposals.motivation: The generalized Maxwell-Bloch equations are fundamental for describing coherent light-matter interactions in complex media, such as graded-index (GRIN) fibers and space-time varying structures. Pre-2026 work has identified rich nonlinear dynamics and instabilities in these systems, but a systematic classification of these instabilities using rigorous dynamical systems theory is lacking. Such an analysis is crucial for designing stable optical amplifiers, pulse shapers, and quantum memory devices based on resonant interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the parameter space of the generalized Maxwell-Bloch system, when coupled to GRIN or space-time boundaries, contains distinct bifurcation structures (e.g., Hopf, saddle-node) that systematically organize the observed instabilities, including those related to temporal reflection and soliton deceleration phenomena.
proposals.method: We will employ a dynamical systems approach, reducing the governing partial differential equations to low-dimensional ordinary differential equation models via a Galerkin-type projection onto appropriate spatial modes of the GRIN medium. Numerical continuation software (e.g., AUTO or MatCont) will be used to trace equilibrium solutions and identify bifurcation points as key parameters (like pump intensity, detuning, and grading index) are varied. Stability will be assessed via linear stability analysis, and the role of the medium's space-time variation, informed by the generalized energy conservation relation, will be incorporated as a time-dependent boundary condition.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a comprehensive stability phase diagram for Maxwell-Bloch systems in complex media, directly linking abstract bifurcation theory to observable optical phenomena like instability thresholds and mode switching. It will provide a predictive framework for instability management in engineered photonic structures, extending the analytical tools available for nonlinear fiber and integrated optics.
proposals.novelty: While bifurcation analysis is standard in simpler optical systems, its application to the full generalized Maxwell-Bloch model within the context of GRIN and space-time varying media—synthesizing Agrawal's recent themes—is novel. The explicit connection between temporal reflection events and specific bifurcation manifolds has not been previously established.

proposals.title: Generalized Variational Framework for Multi-Mode Laser Dynamics in Graded-Index and Space-Time Varying Media
proposals.motivation: Recent work on generalized energy conservation and pulse dynamics in space-time varying media, including graded-index (GRIN) fibers, has primarily focused on single-mode or effective single-mode descriptions. However, practical high-power fiber lasers and complex cavity designs often operate in a multi-longitudinal-mode regime where mode competition, coupling, and nonlinear interactions dictate stability, noise, and spectral properties. A comprehensive theoretical framework for this regime, grounded in variational principles, is currently lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: The generalized Lagrangian density formalism, previously applied to single-pulse propagation in varying media, can be extended to describe the coupled nonlinear dynamics of multiple longitudinal modes, accurately predicting mode-competition-induced instabilities and spectral evolution in GRIN and other complex laser cavities.
proposals.method: We will extend the single-mode variational equations for nonlinear pulse propagation in a dispersive, nonlinear medium with a spatially or temporally varying refractive index. The optical field will be expressed as a sum of orthogonal longitudinal cavity modes with time-dependent amplitudes and phases. A coupled-mode system will be derived by inserting this ansatz into the Lagrangian density and applying Hamilton's principle. Specific analysis will focus on GRIN fiber cavities and cavities with engineered temporal reflections, using parameters from recent experiments. The resulting ordinary differential equations will be solved numerically to study stability basins and transient dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, energy-consistent theoretical model for multi-mode laser dynamics in complex media, directly linking cavity geometry (via index profile) to mode competition outcomes. It will offer new design principles for stabilizing multi-mode lasers or harnessing mode coupling for tailored pulse generation, impacting fields from high-power laser engineering to optical frequency combs.
proposals.novelty: While coupled-mode theory exists, applying the variational formalism—recently advanced for space-time boundaries—to the multi-longitudinal-mode laser cavity problem is novel. It uniquely incorporates energy conservation constraints directly into the mode competition dynamics, moving beyond phenomenological rate equations.

proposals.title: Breakdown of the Averaged Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation for Ultrashort Pulses in Graded-Index Multimode Fibers
proposals.motivation: The generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation (GNLSE) derived via single-mode averaging is the cornerstone for modeling pulse propagation in multimode fibers. Its validity relies on the pulse dispersion length being much longer than the self-imaging (or periodic refocusing) length of the graded-index (GRIN) medium. For emerging high-power, ultrashort-pulse sources, this condition is violated, and the standard averaged model may fail, leading to inaccurate predictions of nonlinear phenomena like supercontinuum generation and soliton dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when the dispersion length of an intense ultrashort pulse becomes comparable to the self-imaging period of a GRIN fiber, the single-mode averaged GNLSE breaks down, and a full spatiotemporal variational model accounting for rapid periodic beam oscillations is required to accurately capture nonlinear pulse evolution and energy exchange.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework based on a vectorial variational approach, treating the full spatiotemporal optical field. The Lagrangian will incorporate the GRIN refractive index profile, material dispersion, and the Kerr nonlinearity. The Euler-Lagrange equations will yield coupled equations for pulse parameters (width, chirp) and beam parameters (spot size, wavefront curvature), linked through the nonlinear term. This model will be solved numerically and compared against full 3D+1 numerical beam propagation method (BPM) simulations and against predictions of the standard averaged GNLSE. Key metrics will include nonlinear phase accumulation, spectral broadening, and spatiotemporal coupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a clear validity criterion for the use of averaged models in GRIN fibers, crucial for designing next-generation multimode nonlinear systems. It will provide a new, more fundamental theoretical tool—a coupled spatiotemporal variational model—for analyzing ultrashort pulses in periodic focusing media, bridging the gap between beam optics and nonlinear pulse propagation theory.
proposals.novelty: While variational methods have been used separately for beams in GRIN media and for pulses in single-mode fibers, their unified application to capture the interplay between fast transverse dynamics and nonlinear temporal evolution in the sub-self-imaging-pulse regime is novel and non-obvious from existing averaged theories.

proposals.title: Variational Analysis of Optical Soliton Dynamics in Graded-Index Media: From Stationary Solutions to Spatiotemporal Reflection
proposals.motivation: Graded-index (GRIN) optical media are fundamental to beam shaping and fiber optics, yet a comprehensive analytical framework for describing soliton dynamics within them remains underdeveloped. While variational methods are well-established for analyzing solitons in Bose-Einstein condensates and homogeneous nonlinear optics, their application to GRIN systems, which feature a continuous transverse refractive index variation, is not fully explored. This gap limits our ability to predict soliton parameters like width, amplitude, and trajectory under complex propagation and reflection conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Lagrangian-based variational approximation, using a Gaussian or sech trial function, can yield closed-form ordinary differential equations for the amplitude, width, and phase of an optical soliton propagating in a GRIN medium. This framework will accurately model the soliton's oscillatory trajectory and its behavior during temporal reflection events induced by rapid index changes.
proposals.method: We will derive the Lagrangian density for the nonlinear Schrödinger equation (NLSE) with a parabolic transverse refractive index profile, n(r) = n0 - n2 r^2. A Gaussian ansatz for the soliton field will be inserted, and the reduced Lagrangian will be integrated over the transverse coordinates. Applying the Euler-Lagrange equations to the integrated Lagrangian with respect to the ansatz's time-varying parameters (amplitude, width, curvature, and position) will yield a set of coupled ODEs. These equations will be solved analytically for stationary solutions and numerically for dynamic propagation, including scenarios mimicking the 'temporal reflection' boundary conditions explored in recent work on space-time varying media.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic variational model for GRIN solitons, delivering analytical expressions for equilibrium parameters and oscillation frequencies. It will establish a direct theoretical bridge between soliton dynamics in GRIN optics and analogous particle-like descriptions in BEC. The model will offer a powerful, intuitive tool for designing GRIN-based devices that exploit soliton self-trapping and controlled reflection.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tailored application of the variational technique, a cornerstone of BEC soliton theory, to the specific context of GRIN optical media, synthesizing Agrawal's recent themes of graded-index physics, pulse propagation, and temporal reflection into a unified analytical framework. This approach is non-obvious as it treats the full spatiotemporal soliton dynamics under a transverse confinement that breaks translational invariance.

proposals.title: Floquet Stability Analysis of Temporal Solitons in Periodically Modulated Nonlinear Media
proposals.motivation: The stability of optical solitons in media with periodic variations in nonlinearity or dispersion is a critical, unsolved problem in nonlinear fiber optics and photonics. Such periodic systems, inspired by graded-index (GRIN) media and space-time varying structures, are key for dispersion management and novel pulse shaping, but their rigorous stability analysis remains limited to heuristic or numerical approaches. A formal framework is needed to predict instability bands and design robust soliton-based devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying Floquet theory to the linear stability equations governing periodic solitons will yield exact stability eigenvalues, revealing distinct instability bands and growth rates that depend on the modulation amplitude and period. This will provide a rigorous predictive tool beyond standard perturbation methods.
proposals.method: We will derive the periodic soliton solution using a variational approach for the nonlinear Schrödinger equation with an oscillating nonlinear coefficient. The linear stability analysis will produce a system of ordinary differential equations with periodic coefficients, to which we will apply Floquet theory. The monodromy matrix will be constructed by numerically integrating the stability equations over one period using a fourth-order Runge-Kutta method, and its eigenvalues will determine stability. This will be implemented for parameters relevant to fiber systems with periodic dispersion or nonlinearity management.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rigorous analytical-numerical framework for stability analysis in periodic nonlinear systems, directly applicable to dispersion-managed and nonlinearity-modulated fibers. It will provide definitive stability maps for solitons in such media, informing the design of stable pulse sources and transmission lines. The method bridges concepts from temporal reflection analysis and generalized energy conservation in varying media.
proposals.novelty: While Floquet theory is standard in linear periodic systems, its direct application to the stability eigenvalue problem of nonlinear periodic solitons is novel. This approach moves beyond the standard linearization with constant coefficients or collective variable theories, offering exact stability spectra for a class of problems previously treated only approximately.

proposals.title: Variational Analysis of Intermodal Nonlinear Dynamics in Multimode Silicon Photonic Waveguides
proposals.motivation: The analysis of nonlinear pulse propagation in silicon waveguides has predominantly relied on the single-mode assumption, neglecting the rich physics of multimode systems. However, practical silicon waveguides, especially wider ones for high-power or dispersion engineering, support multiple spatial modes. Pre-2026 work on graded-index (GRIN) media and space-time varying systems has established frameworks for complex beam dynamics, but a unified theory for intermodal nonlinear coupling—encompassing cross-phase modulation, four-wave mixing, and Raman scattering between modes—in these integrated platforms is lacking, limiting design accuracy and the exploitation of new degrees of freedom.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a variational formalism, extended to include multiple spatial eigenmodes, can effectively model the complex, energy-conserving nonlinear interactions in multimode silicon waveguides, revealing new spatiotemporal soliton families and intermodal energy transfer mechanisms not captured by single-mode theory.
proposals.method: We will develop a coupled-mode framework where the total field is expanded in the waveguide's linear spatial modes. A Lagrangian density will be constructed incorporating the modal fields, the silicon waveguide's nonlinear susceptibility (including Kerr and Raman effects), and dispersion. Applying Hamilton's principle will yield a set of coupled generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equations (GNLSEs) for the modal amplitudes. This system will be solved numerically using a split-step Fourier method adapted for mode coupling. Specific analysis will focus on intermodal soliton formation and the impact of temporal reflection-like phenomena on modal energy redistribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, energy-conserving model for nonlinear multimode dynamics in silicon waveguides, directly extending Dr. Agrawal's work on variational methods and GRIN media to integrated photonics. It will deliver design rules for managing intermodal crosstalk in broadband devices and predict novel nonlinear states exploitable for mode-division multiplexing or all-optical signal processing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing the variational approach for nonlinear pulses with the coupled-mode theory for spatial modes in a lossy, dispersive silicon medium, explicitly challenging the foundational single-mode assumption in silicon nonlinear photonics to model emergent intermodal solitons.

proposals.title: Active Engineering of Free-Carrier Recombination via Ion Implantation for Stable Temporal Soliton Formation in Silicon Photonic Waveguides
proposals.motivation: The formation of optical solitons in silicon waveguides is severely hindered by two-photon absorption (TPA) and the subsequent generation of free carriers, which introduce nonlinear loss and phase modulation that disrupt soliton stability. Pre-2026 research has focused on mitigating these effects through waveguide geometry or operating wavelength, but active control of the free-carrier lifetime itself remains an underexplored avenue for optimizing the nonlinear dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatially selective ion implantation can be used to engineer a controlled reduction in the free-carrier lifetime within a silicon waveguide, thereby suppressing the destabilizing effects of free-carrier dispersion and enabling the formation of stable temporal solitons at telecom wavelengths.
proposals.method: We will fabricate silicon-on-insulator waveguides and use focused ion implantation (e.g., with He+ or Si+ ions) to create defect bands that act as recombination centers, tailoring the carrier lifetime. The nonlinear pulse propagation will be modeled using a generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation extended with a rate equation for free-carrier density, solved via a split-step Fourier method. Experimental validation will involve characterizing the modified lifetime via pump-probe measurements and observing soliton formation and dynamics using an ultrafast laser source and frequency-resolved optical gating (FROG).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel material-engineering protocol for controlling nonlinear optical dynamics in silicon, directly linking defect engineering to soliton physics. It will provide a new pathway to achieve soliton-based functionalities in CMOS-compatible platforms, advancing integrated nonlinear photonics.
proposals.novelty: While ion implantation is a standard process for lifetime control in electronics, its application as a direct tool to engineer the nonlinear optical environment for soliton formation in photonic waveguides is novel. This approach moves beyond passive design to active, post-fabrication tuning of the nonlinear medium's temporal response.

proposals.title: Variational Optimization of Gain-Switching Dynamics in GRIN Fibers for Nonlinear Pulse Shaping
proposals.motivation: Gain-switched semiconductor lasers are a robust source of picosecond pulses, but their output is often chirped and temporally broadened. In pre-2026 fiber optics, the interplay between gain nonlinearity (e.g., gain saturation and recovery dynamics) and propagation in a graded-index (GRIN) medium is not fully exploited for pulse engineering. This work addresses the need for simple, integrated methods to generate shorter, transform-limited pulses directly from gain-switched sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-optimizing the electrical modulation parameters of the gain-switched laser and the dispersive/self-phase modulation properties of a subsequent GRIN fiber segment, the intrinsic gain nonlinearity can be harnessed, rather than mitigated, to produce cleaner, compressed optical pulses.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical model coupling rate equations for a gain-switched laser with a generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation for pulse propagation in a GRIN fiber, incorporating space-time varying index effects. A variational approach, informed by Agrawal's recent work on energy conservation in varying media, will be used to derive reduced equations for pulse parameters. An optimization algorithm (e.g., particle swarm or gradient descent) will then search the parameter space of modulation depth, frequency, and bias current, alongside GRIN fiber length and index profile, to minimize output pulse width and chirp.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a new framework for designing integrated gain-switched pulse sources with improved temporal characteristics. It will yield specific, experimentally verifiable guidelines for modulation and fiber parameters to achieve pulse optimization. The work will extend the concept of 'temporal reflection' and boundary control from pure propagation problems to the active source dynamics domain.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating the gain-switching process and GRIN propagation as a single, co-designed system for nonlinear pulse shaping, using a variational optimization informed by recent theoretical advances in space-time varying media, which is a distinct departure from post-compensation techniques.

proposals.title: Wavefront-Shaped Modal Excitation for Spatially Selective Raman Amplification in Graded-Index Fibers
proposals.motivation: Raman scattering in multimode fibers is highly dependent on the spatial overlap of interacting modes, offering a route to engineer nonlinear gain. However, pre-2026 techniques lack precise, dynamic control over which specific fiber modes are excited by the pump, limiting optimization of Raman processes like amplification or soliton generation. Adaptive optics, proven in microscopy for scattering compensation, provides a powerful tool to address this spatial control problem in fiber optics.
proposals.hypothesis: By using a spatial light modulator (SLM) to perform wavefront shaping of the pump beam, we can selectively and adaptively excite targeted high-order modes in a graded-index (GRIN) fiber, thereby controlling the spatial profile of the Raman gain to optimize nonlinear interactions for specific applications.
proposals.method: We will couple light from a pulsed Raman pump laser into a GRIN fiber via an SLM-based wavefront shaping setup. A stochastic parallel gradient descent (SPGD) algorithm will optimize the SLM phase pattern to maximize the power in a target mode, monitored using a spatially resolved detector or a mode decomposition technique. The shaped pump will then interact with a signal beam in the fiber, and we will measure the resulting modal Raman gain and spectral broadening, comparing it to the case of unshaped (fundamental mode) excitation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct methodology for spatially selective nonlinear control in fibers, moving beyond scalar power optimization. It will provide new experimental data on mode-dependent Raman gain coefficients and demonstrate a novel path to engineer nonlinear pulse propagation, such as controlling the trajectory of Raman solitons through their initial modal composition.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive optics is established in imaging and linear mode control, its application to *nonlinear* modal control for Raman optimization in fibers is novel. This bridges the concepts of temporal reflection in soliton dynamics and the physics of GRIN media with the engineering tool of wavefront shaping.

proposals.title: Enhancing Supercontinuum Flatness and Bandwidth via Dual-Wavelength Pumping and Spatio-Temporal Reflective Control
proposals.motivation: Supercontinuum generation (SCG) in optical fibers, while a cornerstone for broadband sources, often suffers from spectral non-uniformity and limited bandwidth when driven by a single pump wavelength. This is due to the sensitive, phase-matched interplay of nonlinear effects like modulation instability and soliton fission. Pre-2026 work has shown that tailoring the medium's properties, such as in graded-index (GRIN) fibers, can influence this process, but the fundamental seeding dynamics remain constrained by the initial pump condition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that seeding the supercontinuum process with two distinct pump wavelengths, or a spectrally broadened pre-pulse, will provide multiple, phase-matched initiation points for nonlinear spectral broadening, leading to a more uniform and extensive supercontinuum spectrum than single-wavelength pumping.
proposals.method: We will employ a split-step Fourier method to solve the generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation, incorporating the effects of a GRIN fiber's spatially varying refractive index and a modeled temporal reflection boundary. The core experimental architecture involves a dual-wavelength picosecond pump source (e.g., at 1064 nm and 1550 nm) coupled into a highly nonlinear, dispersion-engineered GRIN fiber. We will use a variational approach to analyze the initial pulse propagation and the energy transfer dynamics post the simulated 'temporal reflection' event, which acts as a phase-conjugate boundary to redistribute energy in the time-frequency domain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for deterministic supercontinuum engineering by demonstrating controlled seeding via multi-wavelength initiation. It will yield a practical design principle for flatter, broader spectrum sources critical for optical coherence tomography and spectroscopy. Furthermore, it will extend the concept of energy conservation in space-time varying media to the domain of multi-pump nonlinear interactions.
proposals.novelty: While multi-wavelength pumping has been explored, its systematic combination with GRIN-mediated spatial dynamics and the conceptual framework of temporal reflection for spectral control is novel. This approach moves beyond simple parameter optimization to actively shape the nonlinear process through engineered initial conditions and spatio-temporal boundaries.

proposals.title: Coherence and Noise Dynamics in Mid-Infrared Supercontinuum Generation: A Variational Approach for Spectroscopic Source Optimization
proposals.motivation: Mid-infrared supercontinuum (SC) sources are transformative for molecular spectroscopy, but their utility is critically limited by relative intensity noise (RIN) and degraded temporal coherence, which reduce sensitivity and precision. Existing noise characterizations for near-IR SC are insufficient for the mid-IR regime, where complex nonlinear dynamics in graded-index (GRIN) and chalcogenide fibers, involving soliton fission and dispersive wave generation, introduce distinct stochastic signatures. A predictive framework linking the SC generation process to its fundamental noise properties is urgently needed for engineering high-stability spectroscopic sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the RIN and first-order temporal coherence of a mid-IR SC are governed by the stability of the underlying nonlinear propagation trajectory, which can be quantified and optimized using a generalized, noise-augmented variational model applied to the pulse evolution in a space-time varying effective medium.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework extending Agrawal's recent work on generalized energy conservation and variational methods in space-time varying media. The nonlinear Schrödinger equation for pulse propagation in chalcogenide and GRIN fibers will be coupled to stochastic differential equations modeling quantum and technical noise seeds. A modified variational approach will derive effective equations for key pulse parameters (energy, width, chirp), explicitly including noise-driven perturbations. Numerical simulations using the split-step Fourier method will validate the model against experimental RIN and coherence measurements from a picosecond-pumped, mid-IR SC source, probing the impact of fiber dispersion profile and pump pulse stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive model linking the deterministic and stochastic dynamics of mid-IR SC generation to its measurable noise and coherence metrics. It will provide concrete design guidelines—regarding fiber index profile and pump parameters—for minimizing RIN and maximizing coherence in spectroscopic SC sources. The generalized variational formalism will be a new tool for analyzing noise in complex, space-time varying nonlinear optical systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a stochastic variational framework, informed by recent concepts of temporal reflection and energy conservation in varying media, specifically to the unsolved problem of mid-IR SC noise characterization, moving beyond purely numerical or empirical studies to a predictive, physics-based model.

proposals.title: Intensity Noise Amplification via Spatiotemporal Refractive Index Fluctuations in Graded-Index Media
proposals.motivation: In optical fibers and graded-index (GRIN) media, refractive index fluctuations are primarily known to induce phase noise, limiting coherent systems. However, the dual problem—where such fluctuations directly amplify intensity noise via nonlinear phase-amplitude coupling—remains unexplored. This is critical for high-power pulse propagation and sensing applications where intensity stability is paramount, yet the underlying physics in space-time varying media is not understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deterministic and stochastic spatiotemporal variations in the refractive index of a GRIN medium can resonantly couple to optical pulses, transferring noise from the phase domain to the amplitude domain and significantly enhancing relative intensity noise (RIN) beyond classical expectations.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework extending the generalized energy conservation relation for space-time varying media to include noise coupling. Numerical simulations will model pulse propagation in GRIN fibers using a split-step Fourier method, incorporating randomized index perturbations via the beam propagation method. The variational approach will be employed to derive effective equations for pulse parameters, and the concept of temporal reflection will be used to analyze noise amplification at spatiotemporal boundaries. Laboratory experiments will involve a tunable laser, a GRIN fiber with controlled microbend perturbations, and high-speed photodetectors to measure RIN spectra.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new theoretical link between index fluctuations and intensity noise, providing design principles for noise-suppressing GRIN media. It will deliver quantitative predictions and experimental validation of RIN amplification, impacting the design of stable fiber lasers, high-power delivery systems, and noise-sensitive optical sensors.
proposals.novelty: While phase noise from index variations is well-studied, the systematic exploration of its dual—direct intensity noise amplification via nonlinear coupling in GRIN media—is novel. The application of concepts from temporal reflection and space-time energy conservation to this noise conversion problem represents a significant conceptual advance.

proposals.title: Integrated Optical Limiting and Wavelength Conversion via Two-Photon Absorption in Graded-Index Waveguides
proposals.motivation: On-chip integration of nonlinear optical functions like optical limiting and wavelength conversion is crucial for robust, multi-functional photonic circuits. However, these functions are typically implemented separately, as the strong Kerr nonlinearity needed for parametric processes can be detrimental to stable limiting. Two-photon absorption (TPA) offers a promising nonlinear loss mechanism for limiting but is often considered parasitic for parametric gain. This work seeks to unify these functions in a single device, leveraging TPA's dual role as a nonlinear enabler.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a suitably engineered graded-index (GRIN) waveguide, the intensity-dependent loss from TPA can stabilize and shape optical pulses, enabling effective limiting while simultaneously creating a stable, high-intensity environment that enhances degenerate four-wave mixing for wavelength conversion.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate silicon nitride or chalcogenide glass GRIN waveguides to provide strong transverse confinement and manage dispersion. Using a variational approach, we will derive modified nonlinear Schrödinger equations incorporating TPA and a spatially varying refractive index to model pulse propagation. We will numerically simulate the interplay of TPA, self-phase modulation, and parametric gain, and experimentally characterize the devices using femtosecond pulses, measuring the limiting threshold, converted wavelength efficiency, and output pulse shapes as functions of input intensity and waveguide geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for multi-functional nonlinear photonic devices, demonstrating that a traditionally parasitic nonlinearity (TPA) can be harnessed to enable concurrent functions. It will provide a generalized theoretical framework for pulse evolution in TPA-active, GRIN media, extending conservation relation analyses to dissipative parametric systems.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synthesis lies in intentionally using TPA not just as a loss mechanism, but as a dynamic control for pulse shaping that facilitates, rather than hinders, parametric conversion in an integrated platform, directly connecting the concepts of temporal reflection (nonlinear deceleration) and spatial beam management via GRIN profiles.

proposals.title: Temporal Reflection and Variational Analysis of Quasi-Phase-Matched Soliton Frequency Conversion in Periodically Modulated Silicon Waveguides
proposals.motivation: Silicon photonics lacks a strong second-order nonlinearity, limiting efficient frequency conversion. Quasi-phase-matching (QPM) via periodic poling in lithium niobate is a cornerstone of nonlinear optics, but direct translation to silicon is not possible. This work proposes to engineer an effective QPM condition in silicon by periodically modulating the waveguide width, leveraging its high third-order nonlinearity and dispersion control to enable new integrated nonlinear functionalities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a periodic modulation of a silicon waveguide's width will induce a corresponding periodic modulation of the effective nonlinearity and dispersion, creating an effective quasi-phase-matching condition for parametric processes like soliton-based frequency conversion. This system can be analyzed as a space-time varying medium where the modulation induces a form of 'temporal reflection' for the interacting optical waves.
proposals.method: We will design silicon-on-insulator waveguides with a sinusoidally modulated width, creating a periodic perturbation in the effective refractive index and nonlinear coefficient. Using a variational method applied to the generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation, we will derive coupled-mode equations for the interacting pulses. The analysis will incorporate concepts from graded-index media and the generalized energy conservation relation for space-time varying systems to model the phase-matching dynamics. Numerical simulations will probe the trajectory and conversion efficiency of Raman or parametric solitons under this engineered modulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for achieving quasi-phase-matched nonlinear interactions in conventional silicon waveguides without requiring second-order materials. It will extend the theory of temporal reflection and space-time varying media to engineered, periodic perturbations. The result will be a foundational framework for broadband frequency converters, entangled photon sources, and all-optical signal processors in CMOS-compatible platforms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the conceptual framework of quasi-phase-matching—traditionally for χ(2) crystals—to χ(3)-based silicon waveguides via geometric modulation, and in analyzing this system using the variational formalism and temporal reflection concepts from Agrawal's recent work on space-time varying media.

proposals.title: Variational Optimization of Sub-300-nm Graded-Index Waveguides for Enhanced Soliton Pulse Dynamics via Nonlinear Boundary Effects
proposals.motivation: The drive for ultra-compact, efficient nonlinear photonic devices necessitates waveguides with extreme optical confinement to enhance the Kerr nonlinearity per unit length. However, scaling silicon or silicon-nitride waveguide dimensions below 300 nm introduces significant challenges, including increased scattering loss, pronounced boundary effects, and complex dispersion engineering that can degrade nonlinear pulse propagation. A systematic design framework is needed to harness these nanoscale effects for beneficial nonlinear interactions, moving beyond simple mode area minimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a variational principle, applied to the design of graded-index (GRIN) nanoscale waveguides, can optimally balance field confinement, dispersion, and nonlinearity to enable novel soliton dynamics and enhanced nonlinear efficiency, where the waveguide boundaries play an active role in pulse shaping.
proposals.method: We will design sub-300-nm cross-section silicon nitride waveguides with engineered transverse GRIN profiles using plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition. A variational method, based on the Lagrangian density for the nonlinear Schrödinger equation in a spatially varying medium, will be employed to optimize the index profile for maximal effective nonlinearity γ while managing dispersion. Pulse propagation simulations will incorporate temporal reflection analogs at effective boundaries induced by the strong index contrast. Experimental characterization will involve launching femtosecond pulses and probing nonlinear spectral broadening and soliton dynamics using time-frequency techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for nonlinear nanophotonics by demonstrating that engineered boundaries and GRIN profiles at deep sub-wavelength scales are not merely parasitic but can be leveraged to control light. It will provide a generalized framework for waveguide optimization that unifies concepts of confinement, dispersion, and nonlinearity, leading to more efficient frequency combs and pulse sources on-chip.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a variational optimization, common in nonlinear fiber optics, to the design of extreme-confinement GRIN waveguides, explicitly treating the waveguide boundaries as a functional element for nonlinear pulse propagation rather than a simple constraint.

proposals.title: Variational Optimization of Forward Error Correction for Nonlinear Dispersive Fiber Channels
proposals.motivation: The capacity of long-haul optical fiber communication is fundamentally limited by the interplay of Kerr nonlinearity and dispersion, effects which are deterministic but create complex, data-dependent signal distortions. Current forward error correction (FEC) codes, designed for additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) or linear channels, are suboptimal as they do not account for this structured, nonlinear intersymbol interference. An FEC scheme co-designed with the channel physics is essential to approach the nonlinear Shannon limit.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding a physics-based model of the nonlinear Schrödinger equation (NLSE) channel, including dispersive and nonlinear pulse propagation, into the code design process, we can generate FEC codes whose structure is intrinsically matched to the dominant error mechanisms, yielding superior performance compared to codes designed for a linearized channel.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational framework for code optimization. The channel model will be a split-step Fourier simulation of pulse propagation in a graded-index (GRIN) or standard single-mode fiber. A candidate code (e.g., a spatially-coupled LDPC or a non-binary code structure) will be evaluated by propagating encoded symbol sequences through this model. A variational objective function, combining the generalized energy conservation relation for space-time varying media and the post-FEC bit error rate, will guide a gradient-free optimization (e.g., using genetic algorithms or reinforcement learning) to evolve the code's parity-check matrix or constellation mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of FEC codes with demonstrably higher net coding gain for nonlinear fiber channels. It will provide a concrete methodology for integrating physical-layer propagation models into information-theoretic coding design, moving beyond the standard linear AWGN assumption.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct co-optimization of code parameters with a full NLSE channel simulator using a variational principle, a significant departure from the standard practice of treating nonlinearity as a mere degradation to be compensated separately by digital signal processing.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Graded-Index Fiber Profiles for Target Dispersion Maps via a Variational Optimization Framework
proposals.motivation: Precise dispersion management is critical for high-capacity optical fiber communication and advanced nonlinear pulse propagation, including soliton dynamics. Current fiber design relies on forward modeling of specific index profiles, which is inefficient for achieving complex, target dispersion characteristics, especially in graded-index (GRIN) media. An inverse design methodology would directly address this gap, enabling the rapid creation of fibers tailored for specific dispersion-engineered applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the refractive-index profile of a cylindrical optical fiber can be inversely optimized using a variational calculus framework, such that it yields a pre-specified, wavelength-dependent group-velocity dispersion (GVD) map across a desired bandwidth.
proposals.method: We will formulate the inverse problem as a constrained optimization: minimize the difference between a target GVD curve and the GVD computed from the scalar wave equation for a given axisymmetric index profile n(r). The core algorithm will be a gradient-based optimization, where the gradient of the GVD with respect to n(r) is computed efficiently using an adjoint-variable method derived from the variational principles in Agrawal's recent work. The index profile will be parameterized using a finite set of basis functions (e.g., super-Gaussian or piecewise polynomials), and constraints on realizability (e.g., maximum index contrast, smoothness) will be enforced.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, computational tool for the inverse design of dispersion-managed fibers, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will yield novel, non-intuitive GRIN profiles for flat, anomalous, or specially shaped dispersion curves. The framework will be validated by fabricating and characterizing a designed fiber, demonstrating its utility for nonlinear pulse applications like Raman soliton generation.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of GRIN fibers is mature, applying an adjoint-based variational optimization to inversely engineer the radial index profile for a precise dispersion target is a novel synthesis of computational photonics and fiber optics. This approach directly leverages recent theoretical advances in energy conservation for space-time varying media to solve a longstanding practical design problem.

proposals.title: Atmospheric Graded-Index Lensing and Temporal Boundary Control for Robust Free-Space Optical Links
proposals.motivation: Free-space optical (FSO) communication is limited by atmospheric turbulence, which causes beam distortion and signal loss, analogous to dispersion and loss in early optical fibers. The mature design principles for managing dispersion and nonlinearity in guided graded-index (GRIN) and microstructured fibers have not been systematically applied to their atmospheric dual: a spatially and temporally varying refractive index medium. This work seeks to translate these guided-wave solutions to the unguided domain to stabilize FSO links.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating the turbulent atmosphere as a spatiotemporally graded-index medium with moving boundaries, principles of variational pulse propagation and generalized energy conservation from guided optics can be applied to design pre-compensated beam profiles and adaptive temporal sequences that mitigate fading.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework modeling the turbulent path as a concatenated series of GRIN segments with stochastic parameters, dual to a multi-span fiber link. Using a variational approach, we will derive effective equations for beam width and curvature, treating turbulence-induced phase shifts as a distributed index grating. The concept of 'temporal reflection' from Agrawal's recent work will be adapted to design transmit pulse shapes that pre-compensate for anticipated temporal boundary conditions (e.g., rapid turbulence surges). Numerical simulations will solve the nonlinear Schrödinger equation with stochastic index variations, comparing standard Gaussian beams to our optimized, turbulence-dual profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical bridge between guided and unguided nonlinear optics, yielding concrete design rules for robust FSO transmitters. It will introduce the concept of atmospheric GRIN lensing and temporal boundary control as practical engineering tools, moving beyond post-detection compensation to intelligent pre-distortion based on channel prediction.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious duality lies in treating random atmospheric turbulence not merely as noise, but as a deterministic, though unknown, GRIN medium sequence, allowing the application of variational methods and temporal boundary conditions from fiber soliton physics to a free-space context.

proposals.title: Approximate Self-Similar Propagation in Optical Fibers: Dynamics and Stability Beyond the Exact Compatibility Condition
proposals.motivation: Self-similar parabolic pulses (similaritons) in optical fibers with gain require a strict compatibility condition between dispersion and gain. This condition is rarely met perfectly in practical systems, limiting the applicability of exact analytical solutions. Understanding pulse evolution when this condition is violated is crucial for designing robust amplifiers, lasers, and systems utilizing graded-index (GRIN) or other space-time varying media.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when the exact compatibility condition is relaxed, optical pulses evolve into an approximate self-similar state governed by a perturbed variational principle, leading to stable, quasi-parabolic profiles with quantifiable deviations from ideal similaritons.
proposals.method: We will employ a variational approach, treating the generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation with gain and dispersion as a Lagrangian system. The ansatz will be a chirped parabolic pulse with variable amplitude, width, and chirp parameters. Using the principle of least action, we will derive coupled ordinary differential equations for these parameters under violated compatibility. Numerical simulations (split-step Fourier method) in fiber and GRIN media will validate the analytical model and explore long-term stability and interactions with effects like temporal reflection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalized analytical framework for predicting pulse shapes in non-ideal gain-dispersion landscapes, extending the concept of similaritons. It will yield practical design rules for amplifier systems where the compatibility condition cannot be perfectly engineered and offer new insights into pulse dynamics in complex, space-time modulated media.
proposals.novelty: While exact similariton solutions are well-known, a systematic study of their robustness and the nature of the approximate attractor state under a broken compatibility condition is lacking. The proposed synthesis of a perturbed variational method with concepts from GRIN and temporal reflection physics is novel.

proposals.title: Self-Similar Pulse Propagation in Dispersion-Engineered Silicon Nitride Microresonators
proposals.motivation: Self-similar 'similarton' dynamics are well-established in optical fibers with longitudinally varying dispersion, enabling robust, high-energy pulse propagation. Chip-scale microresonators are a leading platform for frequency combs and integrated photonics, but their pulse dynamics are dominated by discrete, lumped effects. It is unknown if the powerful paradigm of self-similar evolution can be translated to these systems, which would offer a new route to control and stabilize microcombs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering a distributed, graded variation of the group-velocity dispersion along the resonator's waveguide path, akin to a graded-index (GRIN) medium in space, we can induce a stable self-similar propagation regime for pulses within a microresonator.
proposals.method: We will design silicon nitride ring resonators with a waveguide width that adiabatically varies along the ring circumference, creating a prescribed, periodic GVD gradient. Pulse dynamics will be modeled using a generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation incorporating the spatially varying dispersion map and boundary conditions, solved via a split-step Fourier method. The variational approach, central to Agrawal's recent work, will be employed to derive reduced equations for pulse parameters and identify self-similar solutions. Experimental validation will use fabricated devices and a temporal reflection technique to probe the intracavity pulse trajectory.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the theoretical and experimental foundations for similarton dynamics in integrated photonic resonators. It will provide a new framework for designing microcombs with tailored pulse profiles and enhanced stability, directly impacting on-chip metrology and communications. The results will extend the concept of distributed engineering from fibers to resonant systems.
proposals.novelty: While dispersion engineering exists in waveguides, applying it *distributively within a single resonator* to create a self-similar potential is a novel concept. Merging the physics of GRIN-like dispersion landscapes with the boundary-condition constraints of a resonator to find similarton solutions is unexplored.

proposals.title: Dissipative Similaritons in Passive Optical Fibers: Self-Similar Pulse Dynamics Under Distributed Loss
proposals.motivation: Self-similar pulse propagation (similaritons) has been extensively studied in fiber amplifiers where distributed gain drives parabolic pulse formation, yielding high-energy ultrashort pulses with linear chirp. However, the complementary regime—self-similar evolution under distributed loss in passive fibers—remains largely unexplored despite its relevance to long-haul transmission, passive pulse shaping, and understanding fundamental nonlinear wave dynamics. Characterizing dissipative similaritons would complete the theoretical picture of self-similar propagation and could enable novel passive pulse compression and reshaping techniques.
proposals.hypothesis: Optical pulses propagating in passive fibers with distributed linear loss can exhibit a dual class of self-similar solutions whose amplitude decays exponentially while maintaining a parabolic intensity profile and linear chirp, with the similarity scaling laws systematically inverted relative to the gain-driven case described by the nonlinear Schrödinger equation with loss.
proposals.method: We will derive similarity-reduction ansätze for the generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation incorporating distributed loss, normal dispersion, and Kerr nonlinearity to obtain ordinary differential equations governing the self-similar pulse shape, chirp, and temporal width. Numerical split-step Fourier simulations will validate the analytic predictions across a parameter space spanning loss coefficients from 0.2 to 5 dB/km and input peak powers from 0.1 to 10 W. A variational analysis using a parabolic trial function with time-dependent amplitude, width, chirp, and phase will provide approximate closed-form evolution equations, benchmarked against full numerical solutions. We will further investigate the basin of attraction for dissipative similariton formation by launching Gaussian, sech, and super-Gaussian input pulses and quantifying convergence rates to the self-similar profile using a normalized misfit metric.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the existence conditions, scaling laws, and stability properties of dissipative similaritons in passive fibers, providing a theoretical framework dual to the well-known amplifier similariton theory. The results will quantify how loss-driven self-similar dynamics can be exploited for passive parabolic pulse reshaping without requiring active gain media, potentially simplifying pulse-shaping architectures in optical communication and ultrafast laser systems.
proposals.novelty: While similariton theory in fiber amplifiers is well established through the work of Fermann, Kruglov, and others, the systematic treatment of self-similar solutions under distributed loss—including their attraction basin, chirp evolution, and variational description—has not been undertaken, making this a non-trivial dual problem rather than a straightforward sign change in the gain coefficient.

proposals.title: Dissipative Temporal Solitons in Silicon Waveguides via Active Loss Compensation and Spatio-Temporal Boundary Control
proposals.motivation: Silicon photonics is a dominant platform for integration, but its strong two-photon absorption and linear loss have prevented the formation of stable, long-lived optical solitons, which are crucial for on-chip ultrafast processing and frequency combs. The conventional approach relies on passive dispersion and nonlinearity, which is insufficient in lossy silicon. This work addresses the fundamental challenge of loss by inverting the passive waveguide paradigm, moving towards an active, balanced dissipative system.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating distributed or lumped gain sections to compensate for linear and nonlinear losses, and by engineering the spatio-temporal boundaries of the gain regions, a stable dissipative soliton can form in a silicon waveguide, governed by a complex Ginzburg-Landau-type equation.
proposals.method: We will design a silicon waveguide with integrated III-V gain sections, modeled as a longitudinally varying, graded-index (GRIN) gain medium. The pulse dynamics will be analyzed using a variational method applied to a modified nonlinear Schrödinger equation with a complex, space-time varying refractive index profile representing the gain/loss modulation. This approach generalizes the energy conservation relation for varying media to include gain, and we will specifically probe soliton formation and trajectory using concepts of temporal reflection at the boundaries between gain and passive lossy sections.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the theoretical and design framework for dissipative silicon solitons, directly enabling on-chip mode-locked lasers and frequency combs. It will extend the physics of spatio-temporal varying media and boundary interactions to active systems, providing a new paradigm for nonlinear silicon photonics.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synthesis lies in applying the concepts of temporal reflection and graded-index engineering—typically used for beam control in passive media—to actively manage gain and loss boundaries for soliton stabilization in a material (silicon) where they are conventionally considered impossible.

proposals.title: Geometric Bandgap Engineering of Waveguide Dispersion for Deterministic Soliton Wavelength Tuning
proposals.motivation: The generation of optical solitons is critically dependent on achieving anomalous group velocity dispersion (GVD) at a desired wavelength. Standard fiber designs offer limited, fixed dispersion profiles, restricting soliton operation to narrow spectral bands. This limitation hinders applications in mid-infrared spectroscopy and visible-wavelength sensing, where tailored nonlinear light sources are needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating a waveguide's transverse geometry as a tunable photonic unit cell, analogous to acoustic metamaterials, we can computationally design and fabricate waveguides with spatially varying cross-sections to deterministically relocate the anomalous GVD region to a target wavelength, enabling soliton generation at previously inaccessible wavelengths.
proposals.method: We will employ a full-vector finite-element mode solver to map the dispersion landscape as a function of core dimensions and cladding air-hole patterns, inspired by photonic crystal fiber designs. A gradient-based optimization algorithm will iteratively adjust these geometric parameters along the propagation axis to minimize the difference between the calculated zero-dispersion wavelength and the target. Fabrication will utilize the stack-and-draw method for silica fibers and precision drilling/tapering for graded-index (GRIN) waveguides, with dispersion characterized via spectral interferometry and soliton dynamics probed using temporal reflection techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a systematic, geometry-driven framework for dispersion engineering, moving beyond material-based approaches. It will deliver a new class of waveguides capable of generating fundamental solitons at user-specified wavelengths from the visible to the mid-infrared, directly impacting nonlinear frequency conversion and broadband sensing technologies.
proposals.novelty: The approach explicitly adapts the bandgap engineering paradigm from acoustic metamaterials to directly sculpt the optical dispersion curve via geometric tuning, rather than relying solely on nonlinearity management or conventional index grading. This creates a direct analogy between photonic unit cells and their acoustic counterparts for soliton control.

proposals.title: Multi-Objective Robust Design of Soliton Microcombs via Global Sensitivity Analysis of Coupled Thermal-Optical Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Soliton microcombs are critical for applications in spectroscopy, communications, and metrology, but their performance is critically limited by thermal instability and fabrication-sensitive dispersion. Current dispersion engineering often targets a single wavelength, neglecting the coupled thermal-optical dynamics that cause power-induced thermal rollover and soliton collapse, leading to devices with narrow operational margins and poor yield.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying global variance-based sensitivity analysis (Sobol indices) to a coupled thermal-optical nonlinear model will reveal a Pareto-optimal frontier in waveguide geometry, enabling designs that simultaneously maximize the anomalous dispersion bandwidth for soliton tuning and minimize sensitivity to thermal runaway.
proposals.method: We will develop a comprehensive model coupling the Lugiato-Lefever equation for Kerr comb generation with a heat diffusion equation for temperature-dependent refractive index shifts. Using a 2.5D FDTD solver (e.g., Lumerical MODE) and nonlinear envelope equation simulations, we will compute Sobol sensitivity indices for geometric parameters (width, thickness, etch depth, sidewall angle) on key outputs: the integrated anomalous dispersion and the thermal rollover onset power. A multi-objective genetic algorithm (e.g., NSGA-II) will then be employed to identify Pareto-optimal waveguide cross-sections in silicon nitride platforms.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a systematic, physics-informed design framework for robust microcombs, moving beyond single-point dispersion targets. It will produce a ranked sensitivity map of geometric parameters, a set of Pareto-optimal designs for fabrication, and a validated methodology to co-engineer dispersion and thermal stability.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of global sensitivity analysis with dispersion engineering for a multi-objective thermal-optical problem is novel. It directly addresses the critical, yet often decoupled, challenge of thermal instability in microcombs by providing a quantitative, systematic design tool for inherent robustness.

proposals.title: Seismic Deconvolution Methods for Extracting Soliton Content from Nonlinear Silicon Waveguide Pulses
proposals.motivation: Characterizing the soliton content embedded within complex optical pulses propagating through silicon waveguides is essential for designing next-generation photonic signal processors and all-optical communications systems. The standard mathematical tool for this task, the Inverse Scattering Transform (IST), requires solving the Zakharov-Shabat eigenvalue problem—a procedure that is computationally intensive, sensitive to noise, and difficult to apply when two-photon absorption and free-carrier effects in silicon break the integrability conditions. A robust, data-driven alternative that directly operates on experimentally measurable input-output pulse pairs would significantly lower the barrier to soliton-based device characterization.
proposals.hypothesis: The temporal nonlinear transfer of an optical pulse through a silicon waveguide can be modeled as a generalized convolution of the input pulse with the waveguide's nonlinear impulse response, and seismic sparse-spike deconvolution algorithms can invert this model to recover discrete soliton amplitudes, time delays, and dispersive-wave backgrounds with accuracy comparable to the IST under integrable conditions and superior robustness under non-integrable silicon-specific perturbations.
proposals.method: We formulate the output pulse field as a convolution of the input envelope with a sparse temporal reflectivity series, where each spike encodes a soliton's amplitude and group-velocity offset, superposed on a smooth dispersive radiation background. We adapt Wiener deconvolution with a sparsity-promoting L1 penalty (basis pursuit) and iterative reweighted least-squares refinement, borrowing directly from seismic sparse-spike inversion pipelines. Training and validation data are generated by solving the generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation for silicon waveguides—including two-photon absorption, free-carrier dispersion, and Raman effects—using split-step Fourier methods across a parameter space of pulse energies and waveguide lengths. The extracted soliton parameters are benchmarked against IST eigenvalues in the integrable (lossless) limit and against direct spectral-temporal measurements from FROG traces in the non-integrable regime.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces a cross-disciplinary computational framework that reinterprets nonlinear pulse propagation through the lens of geophysical signal processing, providing experimentalists with a practical, noise-tolerant tool for soliton characterization that does not require integrability. It extends Agrawal's foundational treatments of soliton dynamics in optical fibers and graded-index media into silicon photonics, while establishing a formal analogy between seismic reflectivity inversion and nonlinear optical pulse decomposition that may generalize to other waveguide platforms.
proposals.novelty: While seismic deconvolution and optical soliton theory are individually mature fields, the explicit mapping of sparse-spike reflectivity inversion onto the soliton decomposition problem in lossy, non-integrable silicon waveguides has not been explored. This analogy-driven approach circumvents the integrability requirement of the IST and offers a physically interpretable alternative to black-box machine-learning decomposition methods.

proposals.title: Inverse Scattering Transform for Thermal-Optical Solitons in Dense VCSEL Arrays: A Framework for Crosstalk Mitigation
proposals.motivation: The power scaling of densely packed vertical-cavity surface-emitting laser (VCSEL) arrays for applications in optical interconnects and LiDAR is fundamentally limited by thermal crosstalk, which induces complex refractive index variations. Traditional scale analysis of heat diffusion provides a static picture but fails to predict the emergent, self-reinforcing nonlinear pulse dynamics that can arise from the interplay of thermal potentials and optical gain. A fundamental framework to identify and control such detrimental nonlinear states is critically needed to advance array design.
proposals.hypothesis: The combined thermal profile and refractive index distribution in a silicon-based VCSEL array acts as a deterministic potential in a nonlinear Schrödinger equation (NLSE), and its soliton content, decoded via the inverse scattering transform (IST), reveals stable, self-reinforcing thermal-optical soliton states that govern crosstalk dynamics.
proposals.method: We will derive a governing NLSE for pulse propagation in the array, where the thermally induced index change, modeled via scale analysis of the heat equation, enters as a spatially varying potential. The direct scattering problem will be solved numerically using a Fourier collocation method to compute the scattering data (reflection coefficient, bound states). The inverse problem will be implemented via the Zakharov-Shabat algorithm to reconstruct the soliton potentials. This IST analysis will be performed on simulated arrays with varying geometries and driving conditions to map the emergence of bound soliton states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fundamental link between IST theory and thermal crosstalk in laser arrays, yielding a predictive tool for identifying unstable, soliton-driven crosstalk modes. It will establish design principles for array geometries and pulsed driving schemes that suppress these modes, directly enabling higher power density and scalability for silicon photonic systems.
proposals.novelty: While IST is a mature theory for integrable systems, its application to decode emergent solitons from a *non-integrable*, thermally generated potential in a practical laser array is novel. This fusion of IST with thermal scale analysis moves beyond phenomenological models to a first-principles identification of nonlinear crosstalk states.

proposals.title: Inverse Reconstruction of Spatio-Thermal Dynamics in Mode-Locked Lasers via Neural Network Analysis of Optical Spectra
proposals.motivation: In high-power mode-locked fiber lasers and bulk systems, the interplay between nonlinear pulse propagation and thermal effects in the gain medium is critical for stability but difficult to probe directly. Existing diagnostics measure pulse or spectrum separately, failing to capture the coupled spatio-thermal state that governs performance limits and instabilities. A method to non-invasively reconstruct the complete internal temperature profile from a simple spectral measurement would revolutionize laser design and stabilization.
proposals.hypothesis: That a neural network, trained on synthetic data from a coupled nonlinear-thermal model, can accurately invert a single experimental optical spectrum to simultaneously reconstruct the underlying soliton pulse parameters and the radial temperature distribution in the gain medium.
proposals.method: We will generate a large synthetic dataset by numerically solving a system coupling the Generalized Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (for the pulse) with a radially resolved heat diffusion equation (for the medium temperature). A convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, such as a U-Net variant, will be trained on these simulated spectral outputs to map them to the corresponding soliton parameters (order, width, chirp) and 2D temperature maps. The model will be validated against controlled experiments in a GRIN fiber laser system, where temperature can be independently measured via spatially resolved thermography.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, rapid diagnostic tool for complex photonic systems, enabling real-time monitoring of thermal-nonlinear interactions. It will provide deeper physical insight into the stability boundaries of mode-locked lasers and advance the concept of 'temporal reflection' and energy conservation in space-time varying media by directly linking spectral features to internal thermal gradients.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of a physics-based coupled model with neural network inversion to perform 'soliton tomography' is unprecedented. It moves beyond parameter estimation to full spatio-thermal field reconstruction from a single, easily accessible observable—the optical spectrum.

proposals.title: Nonlinearity-Enhanced Slot Waveguides via Resonant Metamaterial Cladding for Femtojoule Soliton Generation
proposals.motivation: Achieving optical solitons in integrated nanophotonics is crucial for on-chip ultrafast signal processing and frequency combs, but the required pulse energies remain prohibitively high (picojoules) due to weak nonlinearities in nanoscale silicon waveguides. This energy barrier limits practical applications in low-power photonic circuits. Drawing an analogy from seismic engineering's tuned mass dampers, we propose a novel waveguide architecture to overcome this limitation.
