proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a slot waveguide, surrounded by a nanostructured metamaterial cladding engineered with resonant optical elements and high nonlinear susceptibility, will act as an efficient nonlinear energy sink, dramatically lowering the threshold pulse energy for soliton formation by concentrating nonlinear interaction in the cladding via evanescent coupling.
proposals.method: We will design a silicon nitride slot waveguide core, surrounded by a cladding of sub-wavelength resonant silicon or III-V semiconductor nanostructures acting as optical 'tuned masses'. Using finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations and coupled-mode theory, we will optimize the geometry and resonance of these elements for maximal evanescent field overlap and nonlinear (Kerr) response. The nonlinear pulse propagation and soliton formation will be modeled using a generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation, incorporating a spatially-dependent nonlinear coefficient derived from the meta-cladding's effective medium response, informed by Dr. Agrawal's recent work on graded-index and space-time varying media.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for engineering nonlinearity in integrated photonics by decoupling strong confinement from the nonlinear response. It is expected to demonstrate a theoretical pathway to sub-100 femtojoule soliton generation in a CMOS-compatible platform. The proposal will provide a general framework for applying 'auxiliary resonator' concepts from other fields to photonic nonlinear engineering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual transfer of the mechanical tuned mass damper principle to photonics to manage nonlinear energy, and in the specific architecture of using a resonant, nonlinear metamaterial as a functional cladding rather than a passive or uniformly nonlinear one. This moves beyond simply enhancing material nonlinearity to designing a dedicated, optically coupled nonlinear subsystem.

proposals.title: Thermo-Optical Co-Design of Silicon Spiral Waveguides for Stable High-Power Soliton Propagation
proposals.motivation: On-chip nonlinear photonic processing, such as soliton-based frequency comb generation, is fundamentally limited by thermal effects. High optical powers induce localized heating in silicon waveguides, altering the refractive index via the thermo-optic effect, which disrupts the delicate balance of dispersion and nonlinearity required for soliton propagation. This thermal instability prevents the scaling of power and interaction length needed for practical, high-performance on-chip devices.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing the waveguide's nonlinear optical properties with integrated, microscale thermal management structures, we can actively extract heat from soliton propagation hotspots, thereby stabilizing the soliton state and enabling significantly higher input powers and longer propagation distances in silicon photonic circuits.
proposals.method: We will fabricate long, spiral silicon nitride waveguides (to minimize two-photon absorption) with embedded thermal vias and on-chip heat spreaders, inspired by GaN power electronics packaging. The thermal design will be guided by a coupled thermo-optical model, extending the variational method and generalized energy conservation relations for space-time varying media to include a temperature-dependent refractive index. We will experimentally probe the soliton dynamics under high-power injection using time-resolved techniques analogous to temporal reflection, measuring the soliton trajectory and stability against thermal perturbation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for thermally robust, high-power nonlinear photonic integrated circuits. It will demonstrate, for the first time, centimeter-scale, stable soliton propagation at watt-level peak powers on a silicon chip, directly enabling high-efficiency Kerr frequency combs and all-optical regenerators. The developed co-design framework will be a critical tool for future high-power photonic systems.
proposals.novelty: The direct integration of active, microscale thermal management from power electronics into the core optical waveguide design is a non-obvious fusion of disciplines. This moves beyond passive thermal mitigation to actively engineer the thermal boundary conditions of the soliton itself, a concept unexplored in pre-2026 nonlinear silicon photonics.

proposals.title: Long-Range Soliton Dynamics and Temporal Reflection in Centimeter-Scale Spiral Silicon Waveguides
proposals.motivation: Silicon photonics offers a compact platform for nonlinear optics, but propagation lengths are typically limited to a few centimeters due to linear loss and two-photon absorption, hindering the study of long-range soliton evolution and interactions. Achieving centimeter-scale propagation on-chip would enable the exploration of soliton physics, such as deceleration and temporal reflection, in a controlled, integrated environment, bridging the gap between fiber systems and chip-scale devices.
proposals.hypothesis: By engineering low-loss, dispersion-tailored spiral silicon waveguides of centimeter scale, we can observe and characterize novel long-range soliton dynamics, including the decelerating trajectory and eventual temporal reflection of Raman-like solitons, governed by a generalized energy conservation relation in the effectively space-time varying medium created by the soliton's own self-interaction.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate silicon-on-insulator waveguides with optimized cross-sections and low sidewall roughness to minimize linear loss. Using graded-index (GRIN) tapering for efficient coupling, light will be launched into compact spiral waveguide geometries achieving >10 cm path length. We will employ a variational method to model pulse evolution, accounting for nonlinearity, dispersion, loss, and the Raman effect. Experiments will use femtosecond pulses from a tunable laser, with time-resolved characterization (e.g., frequency-resolved optical gating) to probe soliton trajectory and potential temporal reflection events.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the first observation of long-range soliton propagation and temporal reflection dynamics in a silicon waveguide, providing a new testbed for nonlinear pulse physics. It will extend the principles of graded-index media and space-time varying interactions to an integrated platform, validating generalized conservation laws for practical on-chip systems.
proposals.novelty: The integration of centimeter-scale spiral waveguides with the study of temporal reflection phenomena is novel, moving beyond short-segment silicon nonlinear optics to explore soliton deceleration and reflection in a monolithic, chip-bound system, which has not been achieved pre-2026.

proposals.title: Controllable Routing of Discrete Spatial Solitons via Engineered Graded-Index Boundaries in Waveguide Arrays
proposals.motivation: Waveguide arrays are a foundational platform for studying discrete diffraction and spatial solitons, with potential applications in all-optical switching and signal processing. However, achieving deterministic, low-power steering and routing of discrete solitons within a network remains a challenge, limiting their functional integration. This work addresses this by leveraging graded-index (GRIN) engineering and boundary control, concepts central to the researcher's recent investigations, to create a novel routing architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by introducing a transversely graded refractive index profile and tailored boundary reflections within a one-dimensional waveguide array, the trajectory of a discrete spatial soliton can be predictably controlled and switched between output channels via its initial launch conditions and power.
proposals.method: We will design and model a one-dimensional array of coupled, nonlinear waveguides with a superimposed transverse GRIN profile, creating an effective potential landscape. The boundaries will be engineered using a spatially varying refractive index taper, inspired by temporal reflection concepts, to act as controllable reflectors. The system will be analyzed using a discrete nonlinear Schrödinger equation framework, employing a variational approach to derive effective particle-like equations of motion for the soliton's center. Numerical simulations will test routing protocols by varying initial beam position, angle, and power.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for soliton routing based on GRIN-induced potentials and engineered boundaries, moving beyond static lattice defects. It will provide a theoretical framework and design principles for integrated optical circuits that use discrete solitons as reconfigurable information carriers, directly contributing to the physics of beam control in space-time varying media.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of GRIN media engineering and boundary reflection control—two of the researcher's core themes—specifically for steering discrete solitons, a problem typically addressed with uniform arrays and static nonlinearity management. This creates a dynamic, tunable routing mechanism distinct from previous approaches.

proposals.title: Neural Network Prediction and Optimization of Soliton Dynamics in Graded-Index and Space-Time Varying Media
proposals.motivation: Predicting and controlling soliton propagation in complex photonic structures like graded-index (GRIN) fibers and space-time varying media is crucial for advanced optical communications and signal processing. Traditional variational methods or direct numerical solutions are computationally expensive or lack generality for rapid design and optimization of such systems, especially when considering novel effects like temporal reflections.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep neural network trained on a dataset generated from the nonlinear Schrödinger equation and its generalizations can accurately and rapidly predict soliton evolution parameters (e.g., pulse width, amplitude, trajectory) in complex media, and this model can be inverted to discover optimal medium parameters for desired soliton outcomes.
proposals.method: We will generate a large synthetic dataset by solving the generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation for GRIN and space-time modulated media using a split-step Fourier method, varying initial soliton parameters (power, width, chirp) and medium properties (index profile, modulation strength). A convolutional neural network (CNN) will be trained to map initial conditions and medium profiles to the soliton's output state. Subsequently, a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) or a reinforcement learning agent will be employed to solve the inverse problem: finding the optimal medium parameters (e.g., GRIN profile period, modulation depth) to achieve a target soliton behavior, such as stable propagation or a specific deceleration profile.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fast, data-driven surrogate model for soliton dynamics in complex media, significantly accelerating the design of novel photonic devices. It will establish a machine-learning framework for the inverse design of optical structures tailored for specific nonlinear pulse propagation tasks, bridging numerical physics and computational optimization.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been applied to nonlinear optics, its targeted integration with the specific physics of temporal reflections and GRIN media—core themes of Agrawal's recent work—for soliton parameter optimization represents a novel synthesis. The approach moves beyond pure prediction to the optimization of the medium itself based on a learned model of soliton dynamics.

proposals.title: Beyond the Linewidth Enhancement Factor: A Generalized Model for Wavelength- and Intensity-Dependent Nonlinear Refraction in Graded-Index and Dispersive Media
proposals.motivation: The standard linewidth enhancement factor (alpha-factor) model for semiconductor lasers and nonlinear optics treats the nonlinear refractive index as a simple, wavelength-independent constant scaled from the gain. This is a significant oversimplification, especially in broadband and high-intensity pulse propagation in graded-index (GRIN) and dispersive fibers, where both the wavelength dependence and the intensity dependence of n2 critically affect soliton dynamics, modulation instability, and beam self-focusing. A more complete model is needed to accurately describe phenomena like temporal reflection and pulse deceleration observed in recent experiments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating explicit wavelength-dependent and intensity-dependent contributions to the nonlinear refractive index into a generalized propagation model will reveal new dynamical regimes and correct quantitative predictions for pulse evolution and beam stability in GRIN and other space-time varying media, which are missed by the single alpha-factor approximation.
proposals.method: We will develop a generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation (GNLSE) framework where the nonlinear term n2(I, λ) is modeled using a Taylor expansion in intensity and a Laurent expansion in wavelength around a carrier, informed by known material dispersion of the Kerr effect. This model will be analyzed using variational methods to derive reduced equations for pulse parameters (width, chirp, amplitude). We will implement numerical simulations using a split-step Fourier method with this generalized n2 in both uniform and GRIN fiber geometries, comparing results to the standard alpha-factor model and to experimental data on Raman soliton deceleration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational theoretical framework for treating complex nonlinear refraction beyond the alpha-factor, directly applicable to pulse propagation in engineered GRIN media and ultrafast photonics. It will yield new criteria for soliton stability and beam collapse, and offer a more accurate tool for interpreting experiments on temporal reflection and pulse trajectory control in space-time varying index structures.
proposals.novelty: While the limitations of the alpha-factor are known, a systematic formulation of a wavelength- and intensity-dependent n2 model integrated into a variational treatment of pulse propagation in GRIN media is novel. This directly connects Agrawal's recent work on temporal reflection and GRIN physics to a more fundamental description of the nonlinearity itself.

proposals.title: Efficient Modeling of Ultrashort Pulse Propagation in Semiconductor Optical Amplifiers Using a Generalized Split-Step Fourier Method
proposals.motivation: Semiconductor optical amplifiers (SOAs) are critical for integrated photonic circuits and high-speed optical communications, but their complex nonlinear dynamics, including gain saturation, carrier heating, and spectral hole burning, make accurate pulse propagation modeling computationally intensive. Existing finite-difference methods are often too slow for simulating long amplifier chains or optimizing device designs. An efficient, high-accuracy numerical tool is urgently needed for the design of next-generation SOA-based systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the split-step Fourier method (SSFM), widely used for modeling nonlinear pulse propagation in optical fibers, can be effectively generalized and adapted to model the spatio-temporal dynamics in SOAs, offering a significant gain in computational efficiency while maintaining physical accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop a generalized SSFM algorithm tailored for SOAs by incorporating the amplifier's gain dynamics into the nonlinear operator. The propagation equation will be derived from the nonlinear Schrödinger equation, modified with a time-dependent gain coefficient governed by carrier rate equations. The linear operator will handle dispersion and linear loss, while the nonlinear operator will integrate gain saturation, self-phase modulation, and carrier-induced index changes. The algorithm will be implemented with an adaptive step-size control to balance accuracy and speed, and validated against established finite-difference beam propagation method (FD-BPM) results for known SOA configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the optics community with a fast, robust numerical tool for SOA pulse propagation simulation, enabling the exploration of complex regimes like ultra-short pulse amplification and SOA-based pulse shaping. It will establish a formal computational bridge between fiber optics and semiconductor amplifier modeling, extending the utility of the SSFM to active, dispersive, and highly nonlinear integrated media.
proposals.novelty: While the SSFM is standard in fiber optics, its direct application to semiconductor amplifiers—where the 'nonlinearity' is dominated by dynamic, saturable gain rather than an instantaneous Kerr effect—is non-trivial and represents a novel synthesis of Prof. Agrawal's expertise in beam propagation methods, pulse dynamics, and graded-index media with active device modeling.

proposals.title: Phase-Matched Four-Wave Mixing in Graded-Index Semiconductor Waveguides via Fiber-Inspired Dispersion Engineering
proposals.motivation: Four-wave mixing (FWM) in optical fibers is a mature nonlinear process whose efficiency depends critically on phase matching and dispersion management. Semiconductor waveguides offer orders-of-magnitude larger nonlinear coefficients than silica fibers, yet achieving efficient broadband FWM in these structures remains challenging due to strong normal dispersion and tight mode confinement. Transferring the dispersion-engineering and phase-matching strategies perfected in fiber optics to semiconductor platforms could unlock compact, chip-scale wavelength converters and parametric amplifiers operating at low pump powers.
proposals.hypothesis: By designing graded-index (GRIN) transverse refractive-index profiles in semiconductor waveguides—analogous to GRIN fiber designs—one can tailor the group-velocity dispersion to satisfy the phase-matching condition for degenerate and non-degenerate FWM across a bandwidth exceeding 10 THz, achieving conversion efficiencies comparable to kilometer-length fibers in millimeter-scale devices.
proposals.method: We will use a variational approach, extending Agrawal's GRIN media framework, to derive analytic dispersion relations for graded-index AlGaAs and silicon nitride ridge waveguides with parabolic and higher-order index profiles. A split-step Fourier method will solve the coupled nonlinear Schrödinger equations governing pump, signal, and idler fields, incorporating two-photon absorption, free-carrier effects, and boundary reflections at waveguide facets. Parametric sweeps over core width, index contrast, and profile exponent will map the phase-mismatch landscape and identify optimal geometries. Predictions will be validated against published experimental FWM spectra in straight and serpentine semiconductor waveguides.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish quantitative design rules that translate fiber-optic dispersion management principles into semiconductor waveguide geometries, providing closed-form criteria for the index profile parameters that maximize FWM bandwidth and efficiency. The variational-analytic framework will reduce the computational cost of waveguide optimization compared to full numerical mode solvers, enabling rapid prototyping of next-generation integrated parametric devices.
proposals.novelty: While prior semiconductor FWM studies rely on geometry-only dispersion engineering (width, height, slot), this proposal introduces continuously graded transverse index profiles as a new degree of freedom, directly importing and adapting the GRIN fiber formalism that Agrawal has developed for free-space and fiber contexts into on-chip photonics.

proposals.title: Coupled-Mode Perturbation Theory for Four-Wave Mixing in Semiconductor Optical Amplifiers: A Variational Approach
proposals.motivation: Semiconductor optical amplifiers (SOAs) are crucial for integrated photonic signal processing, but their nonlinear wave mixing dynamics are complex due to strong dispersion, gain saturation, and refractive index changes. Existing models, often based on numerical integration of the nonlinear Schrödinger equation, lack the analytical insight provided by perturbation methods common in bulk nonlinear crystal theory. A rigorous coupled-mode framework for SOAs would enable clearer design rules for wavelength conversion and parametric amplification in chip-scale systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The coupled-mode theory formalism, foundational for describing parametric processes in lossless, dispersion-engineered crystals, can be generalized to describe four-wave mixing in active, dispersive SOA waveguides by incorporating a variational principle that accounts for gain, carrier-induced nonlinearity, and the spatiotemporal boundaries of the amplified pulse.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Lagrangian-based coupled-mode theory for χ(2) and χ(3) bulk crystals to the SOA context. Starting from a nonlinear propagation equation with a complex, intensity-dependent refractive index (incorporating gain and Kerr nonlinearity), we will derive a variational principle. The optical field will be expressed as a superposition of orthogonal modes (e.g., Hermite-Gauss) of a reference graded-index (GRIN) waveguide. The evolution of the complex amplitudes of these modes will be governed by coupled equations derived via the Euler-Lagrange equations, treating the SOA's nonlinearity and gain as a perturbation to the linear GRIN medium. This approach directly extends the 'generalized energy conservation' concepts from space-time varying media to an active system.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a new analytical tool for predicting conversion efficiency and bandwidth of SOA-based wave mixers, linking device parameters (gain, confinement, dispersion) directly to performance metrics. It will provide a theoretical bridge between the well-established domain of passive nonlinear optics and active semiconductor photonics, offering a framework to engineer 'temporal reflections' and pulse shaping via controlled nonlinear interactions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a variational, boundary-aware coupled-mode perturbation theory—typically used for conservative systems—to the non-conservative, gain-driven environment of an SOA, explicitly connecting the physics of GRIN media and temporal reflections to the modeling of nonlinear wave mixing in amplifiers.

proposals.title: Dynamic Brillouin Grating Stopband Engineering via Spatially-Varying Acoustic Velocity Profiles
proposals.motivation: Brillouin dynamic gratings (BDGs) are powerful tools for distributed sensing and signal processing, but their performance relies on a fixed Brillouin frequency shift (BFS). In real-world applications, temperature and strain variations along the fiber create a spatially inhomogeneous BFS, detuning the grating's stopband and degrading its reflectivity and spectral selectivity. This limits the practical utility of BDGs in non-laboratory environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by actively mapping the local BFS and using it to design a compensating spatial variation in the pump wavevectors, a stable, high-reflectivity stopband can be synthesized in a fiber with arbitrary temperature/strain gradients.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational framework to model the BDG in a medium with a spatially varying acoustic velocity, extending Agrawal's generalized energy methods. The local BFS will be profiled using Brillouin optical time-domain analysis (BOTDA). This profile will then inform the synthesis of counter-propagating pump pulses with tailored phase fronts, generated via a programmable spectral shaper, to create a longitudinally chirped BDG that realigns the stopband. Numerical simulations based on coupled-mode equations will guide the experimental implementation in graded-index (GRIN) and standard fibers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational model and experimental method for engineering robust Brillouin gratings in inhomogeneous media. It will directly enable more reliable distributed fiber sensors and tunable delay lines that are immune to environmental cross-talk. The derived variational principle will unify the description of wave interactions in space-time varying opto-acoustic media.
proposals.novelty: While BFS variation is a known problem, prior work treats it as a detrimental perturbation. Our approach novelly treats the inhomogeneity as a design parameter, using active pump shaping to 'pre-compensate' the grating, transforming a liability into a tool for stopband engineering.

proposals.title: Suppression of Stimulated Brillouin Scattering in Continuous-Wave High-Power Fiber Systems via Spatio-Temporal Index Modulation
proposals.motivation: Stimulated Brillouin Scattering (SBS) is the primary power-limiting nonlinearity in narrow-linewidth, continuous-wave (CW) high-power fiber amplifiers and lasers, crucial for applications in directed energy, industrial processing, and scientific research. Existing suppression techniques, such as thermal or strain gradients, often add complexity or are insufficient for extreme power scaling. Extending the fiber Bragg grating (FBG)-based suppression scheme, demonstrated for Q-switched pulses, to CW operation addresses a critical technological bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: A precisely designed, longitudinally varying refractive index profile, inspired by graded-index (GRIN) and FBG concepts, can create a distributed, phase-mismatched spatio-temporal boundary that suppresses the coherent build-up of the Brillouin Stokes wave under CW excitation, significantly raising the SBS threshold.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework using a variational approach and coupled-mode theory for the interaction between the CW pump and the Stokes wave in a medium with a deliberately engineered axial index variation n(z). This builds on the researcher's recent work on generalized energy conservation in space-time varying media. The core method involves numerically optimizing the n(z) profile—using algorithms like simulated annealing or genetic algorithms—to maximize the Brillouin gain bandwidth and minimize peak gain. The design will be validated through full-wave simulations of the nonlinear coupled amplitude equations, incorporating the reflected and boundary effects of the index modulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, passive, and all-fiber compatible method for SBS suppression in CW regimes, directly enabling higher output powers. It will yield a new theoretical model for nonlinear interactions in longitudinally inhomogeneous optical media, bridging concepts from GRIN physics, temporal reflection, and nonlinear fiber optics.
proposals.novelty: While index variations have been studied for pulse propagation and Q-switched SBS suppression, their application to fundamentally alter the CW SBS gain spectrum via deterministic spatio-temporal phase mismatch is unexplored. This moves beyond simple apodization to a holistic engineering of the medium's response to a continuous stimulus.

proposals.title: A Generalized Nonlinear Parameter for Multi-Wave Interactions in Graded-Index and Step-Index Fibers
proposals.motivation: The standard nonlinear coefficient γ is derived for a single mode and is insufficient for describing systems where self-phase modulation (SPM), cross-phase modulation (XPM), and four-wave mixing (FWM) interact strongly and simultaneously. This limitation is critical for modern high-capacity fiber systems using multi-core, multi-mode, or space-division multiplexing, as well as for supercontinuum generation in graded-index (GRIN) fibers, where the interplay of nonlinear effects is complex and mode-dependent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the nonlinear response of an optical fiber under simultaneous SPM, XPM, and FWM can be characterized by a generalized, tensorial nonlinear parameter, Γ_ijkl, which depends on the spatial overlap of the interacting fields and the fiber's refractive index profile, moving beyond the scalar γ approximation.
proposals.method: We will employ a variational approach, extending the Lagrangian formalism used for pulse propagation in nonlinear media, to derive coupled amplitude equations for multiple interacting waves. The generalized nonlinear overlap integrals will be computed numerically for specific GRIN and step-index fiber profiles. We will validate the model by comparing its predictions with full numerical solutions of the nonlinear Schrödinger equation and with controlled experiments using pulsed sources in fabricated GRIN fibers, measuring spectral broadening and FWM efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous theoretical framework and a practical metric for quantifying and comparing nonlinear performance in advanced fiber designs. It will enable more accurate modeling of nonlinear signal degradation in mode-division multiplexed systems and offer new design principles for nonlinear fiber devices based on engineering the generalized Γ parameter.
proposals.novelty: While γ is well-established for single-mode fibers, defining a generalized parameter that captures the tensorial nature of multi-wave nonlinear interactions in complex index profiles is novel. This directly builds on Agrawal's recent work on GRIN media and temporal reflections but applies it to the fundamental characterization of nonlinearity itself.

proposals.title: Variational Optimization of Nonlinear Pulse Dynamics in Hybrid Glass Fibers via Joint Dispersion-Nonlinearity Engineering
proposals.motivation: Advanced nonlinear fiber optics applications, from supercontinuum generation to parametric amplification, require simultaneous control over dispersion and nonlinearity. Conventional fibers offer limited degrees of freedom, making it difficult to achieve tailored dispersion profiles (e.g., flattened, low anomalous) alongside a high nonlinear coefficient (γ). Hybrid fibers combining multiple glass materials, such as silica and soft glasses, present a promising but underexplored design space for co-optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically combining glasses with complementary optical properties in a hybrid core/cladding or multi-segment transverse structure, one can independently engineer the waveguide dispersion and the material nonlinearity to achieve unprecedented combinations of dispersion slope and nonlinear parameter, enabling new regimes of pulse propagation and soliton dynamics.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled variational-boundary approach. First, a finite-element mode solver will be used to compute the dispersion (β(ω)) and effective nonlinearity (γ) for hybrid fiber designs with spatially varying refractive index profiles incorporating high-nonlinearity glasses (e.g., chalcogenides). A multi-parameter optimization algorithm, guided by a Lagrangian derived from the nonlinear Schrödinger equation, will then be applied to co-optimize target dispersion curves and γ. The designs will be validated by simulating pulse propagation using a split-step Fourier method, with a focus on phenomena like soliton fission and temporal reflection at engineered interfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a systematic framework for designing hybrid fibers that break the conventional trade-offs between dispersion and nonlinearity. It will deliver specific, manufacturable fiber blueprints for applications in ultrafast optics and lead to a generalized understanding of pulse evolution in media with jointly engineered linear and nonlinear responses.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid fibers exist, a systematic co-optimization of dispersion and nonlinearity using a variational formalism applied to the full transverse structure is novel. This moves beyond simple parameter tuning to a holistic design paradigm informed by fundamental pulse propagation physics.

proposals.title: Effective Nonlinearity Enhancement in Optical Fibers via Metamaterial-Inspired Graded-Index Microstructures
proposals.motivation: The nonlinear optical response of conventional silica fibers is inherently weak, limiting the efficiency of nonlinear processes like soliton generation and frequency conversion without impractically long interaction lengths or high peak powers. Pre-2026 effective-medium theories for electromagnetic metamaterials demonstrate that composite structures can exhibit optical properties not found in nature, offering a pathway to engineer enhanced effective nonlinear coefficients.
proposals.hypothesis: By designing a composite fiber core with a fine-scale, longitudinally invariant graded-index (GRIN) microstructure of alternating high- and low-nonlinearity materials, we can derive an effective nonlinear parameter (γ_eff) significantly exceeding that of a homogeneous fiber made from the constituent materials, while maintaining acceptable linear loss and dispersion properties.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-scale homogenization approach, treating the microstructure's fast spatial variations analytically to derive effective nonlinear and linear wave equations for the slow envelope. The method involves solving the underlying variational problem for the electromagnetic field in the periodic GRIN medium, akin to the generalized energy conservation relations for space-time varying media. We will then use beam propagation method (BPM) simulations on the effective medium model to study pulse propagation and soliton dynamics, comparing results with full-vector finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations of the actual microstructure to validate the theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a formal framework for applying metamaterial concepts to nonlinear fiber optics, providing new design principles for compact nonlinear fiber devices. It will yield specific, manufacturable blueprints for composite fibers with tailored, enhanced nonlinearity for applications in supercontinuum generation and all-optical signal processing.
proposals.novelty: While effective-medium theories are established for linear metamaterials, their rigorous application to engineer *enhanced effective nonlinearity* in guided-wave optics, particularly by leveraging GRIN microstructures within a fiber geometry, is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of Prof. Agrawal's expertise in pulses, variational methods, and GRIN media.

proposals.title: Inverse Spectral Reconstruction of Nonlinear and Dispersive Fiber Parameters via Variational Optimization and Temporal Reflection Probes
proposals.motivation: Characterizing the nonlinear and dispersive properties of optical fibers, especially graded-index (GRIN) and microstructured fibers, is critical for system design but often requires multiple, complex direct measurements. An inverse method that infers these parameters from a simple output spectrum would enable rapid, non-destructive fiber profiling and quality assessment, accelerating the development of advanced fiber-based devices and communication systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The complex spectral signature of a known input pulse after propagation through a fiber contains sufficient information to uniquely reconstruct the fiber's key parameters—including nonlinear coefficient (γ), dispersion profile (β_n), and any longitudinal variations—by solving an inverse problem constrained by generalized energy conservation relations.
proposals.method: We will formulate a variational optimization framework where the forward model is the Generalized Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (GNLSE) for a GRIN or uniform medium. The cost function will be the L2-norm difference between simulated and measured output spectra. Gradients with respect to the unknown parameters (γ(z), β_2(z), β_3, etc.) will be computed using the adjoint method. To enhance sensitivity, we will employ temporal reflection phenomena—inspired by recent work on space-time varying media—as a deliberate probe by introducing a known longitudinal perturbation, creating distinct spectral features that constrain the inverse problem.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust computational tool for single-shot, spectrum-based fiber characterization. It will extend the physics of temporal reflection from a diagnostic tool to an active component of an inversion algorithm. The derived methodology will provide a new standard for inferring spatially varying parameters in complex waveguides.
proposals.novelty: While inverse problems for simple fibers exist, the combined use of a variational adjoint-based inversion specifically for GRIN/media parameters from spectra alone, augmented by engineered temporal reflection probes to break degeneracies, is a novel synthesis of Prof. Agrawal's recent themes in boundary effects, graded-index physics, and generalized conservation laws.

proposals.title: Variational Design of Graded-Index Fibers for Optimal Nonlinearity Suppression via a Space-Time Dual Problem
proposals.motivation: In high-power linear optical systems, such as telecom amplifiers and precision metrology sources, unwanted nonlinear effects like self-phase modulation degrade signal fidelity and limit power scaling. Current fiber designs for nonlinearity management are often ad-hoc or rely on simple large-mode-area geometries, lacking a fundamental optimization principle. A systematic approach to engineer the optical medium itself for maximal nonlinear suppression is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The principles governing nonlinearity enhancement in tailored media, such as through engineered dispersion or confinement, can be inverted within a variational framework to define and solve a 'dual' optimization problem for designing fiber profiles that fundamentally minimize nonlinear phase accumulation.
proposals.method: We will employ a variational calculus approach, treating the fiber's refractive index profile n(r) as the function to be optimized. The objective functional will be derived from the generalized energy conservation relation for space-varying media, constraining it to maintain single-mode operation and target dispersion. Using a Lagrangian formulation, we will derive the Euler-Lagrange equation for the index profile that minimizes the effective nonlinear coefficient γ. This will be solved numerically for specific wavelength and power targets, focusing on graded-index (GRIN) and multi-layer core designs, with validation via beam propagation method (BPM) simulations of pulse evolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles methodology for designing 'anti-nonlinear' fibers, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will yield concrete, manufacturable GRIN profiles that theoretically suppress nonlinearity beyond conventional step-index fibers. The derived dual problem will formally connect the physics of nonlinear enhancement and suppression within a single theoretical framework.
proposals.novelty: While GRIN fibers are studied for dispersion management, applying a variational optimization directly to minimize nonlinearity by inverting the enhancement problem is a novel conceptual shift. The approach integrates Agrawal's recent work on temporal reflection and energy conservation in varying media into a unified design strategy for linear systems.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Graded-Index Fibers for Space-Division Multiplexing via the Manakov Equation
proposals.motivation: Space-division multiplexing (SDM) is critical for scaling optical network capacity, but inter-channel nonlinearities, governed by the Manakov equation, impose a fundamental limit. Current fiber design is largely heuristic, optimizing for low differential mode delay (DMD) but not directly for nonlinear performance. An inverse framework that derives fiber parameters from a desired Manakov nonlinear coupling matrix is needed to systematically minimize nonlinear penalties.
proposals.hypothesis: The effective nonlinear coefficient matrix in the Manakov equation for a multimode or multicore fiber can be used as a target for an inverse optimization problem, yielding practical graded-index (GRIN) fiber designs with significantly reduced inter-modal nonlinear penalties compared to standard designs.
proposals.method: We will formulate a constrained inverse problem where the target is a Manakov nonlinear matrix with minimized off-diagonal coupling. The forward model computes this matrix from the transverse field profiles and refractive index distribution, n(x,y), using coupled-mode theory. We will employ a variational optimization loop, treating n(x,y) as a continuous function parameterized by a GRIN profile (alpha, delta-n) and core geometry. A gradient-based algorithm, informed by adjoint sensitivity analysis derived from the generalized energy conservation relations for varying media, will iteratively adjust parameters to match the target nonlinear matrix while respecting constraints on DMD, attenuation, and manufacturability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a systematic, physics-driven methodology for designing SDM fibers with inherent low nonlinear crosstalk. It will deliver specific, optimized GRIN fiber parameter sets and a new computational tool linking fundamental nonlinear wave propagation constraints to practical engineering design.
proposals.novelty: While the Manakov equation is widely used to model nonlinearities in SDM, inverting it to prescribe fiber geometry is a non-obvious and unexplored inverse problem. The approach uniquely integrates the variational treatment of beam propagation in GRIN media with an inverse design paradigm for nonlinear optics.

proposals.title: Simplified Manakov Equations for Two- and Three-Mode Graded-Index Fibers: A Variational Approach
proposals.motivation: The propagation of short optical pulses in few-mode fibers is governed by coupled generalized Manakov equations, which are complex due to numerous inter-modal nonlinear coefficients. For practical system design in space-division multiplexing and nonlinear optics, simpler models are essential. This work addresses the need for maximally simplified yet accurate equations for the dominant two- and three-mode cases in graded-index (GRIN) fibers, a common practical medium.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a variational principle and leveraging the specific spatial mode structure and symmetry of standard GRIN fibers, the full set of nonlinear coupling coefficients can be reduced to a single effective parameter per mode group, yielding a simplified Manakov system that retains accuracy for pulses with realistic bandwidths.
proposals.method: We will derive the equations from a Lagrangian density for the pulse envelopes in a GRIN medium. Using the known Hermite-Gaussian or Laguerre-Gaussian spatial modes of the parabolic index profile, we will analytically compute all nonlinear overlap integrals. A key step is to systematically apply the 'generalized energy conservation relation for space-time varying media' to identify dominant terms and permissible simplifications that conserve energy in the simplified model. The final simplified equations will be validated against full numerical simulations of the unsimplified system for soliton and pulse collision scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a closed-form, tractable model for nonlinear pulse propagation in practical few-mode fibers, directly usable for engineering analyses of inter-modal nonlinearity. It will clarify the fundamental nonlinear coupling structure in GRIN fibers and establish the limits of the single-parameter approximation, impacting the design of multimode soliton systems and nonlinear mode coupling devices.
proposals.novelty: While the full Manakov system is known, its rigorous reduction for specific, practical fiber index profiles using a variational framework and symmetry arguments is unexplored. The application of recent theoretical conservation tools to justify and bound the simplifications is a novel methodological step.

proposals.title: Spatial Optimization of Raman Pump Power for Enhanced Soliton Control in Graded-Index Fibers
proposals.motivation: In Raman amplification and soliton dynamics, the standard assumption of a uniform pump power distribution along the fiber is a significant simplification that limits performance. Pre-2026 work on space-time varying media and temporal reflection has highlighted the critical role of longitudinal variations in medium properties for pulse manipulation. This proposal directly challenges this assumption to unlock new regimes of pulse control and efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a strategically designed, non-uniform longitudinal profile of Raman pump power, acting as a tailored space-time varying gain medium, will enable superior control over soliton trajectories, amplification efficiency, and nonlinear spectral dynamics compared to a uniform pump.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational framework, extending Agrawal's generalized energy conservation relation, to derive coupled equations for pulse parameters and pump power evolution. The pump profile will be treated as a control function within an optimization loop, using a gradient-descent algorithm to minimize a cost function (e.g., target soliton delay or amplified signal noise). Numerical simulations will model pulse propagation in a graded-index (GRIN) fiber, with the optimized pump profile implemented via a programmable multi-wavelength pump source and variable optical attenuators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic methodology for designing Raman pump profiles for specific nonlinear optical tasks. It will yield new physical insights into soliton dynamics in actively shaped gain landscapes and demonstrate a practical engineering approach to enhance the performance of fiber-based amplifiers and pulse shapers.
proposals.novelty: While pump power optimization has been studied for noise figure, applying variational principles and temporal reflection concepts to explicitly design a non-uniform pump as a control parameter for soliton trajectory engineering in GRIN fibers is a novel synthesis of the researcher's recent themes.

ideas.title: Spectral Deconvolution for Silicon Solitons via Seismic Signal Processing Analogy
ideas.description: This idea applies the core methodology of seismic signal deconvolution from geophysics to the problem of characterizing soliton content in silicon waveguide pulses. In seismology, a recorded seismic trace is modeled as the convolution of a source wavelet with the Earth's reflectivity series; inverse methods are then used to deconvolve the source and recover the subsurface structure. Analogously, the complex optical pulse emerging from a nonlinear silicon waveguide can be treated as the convolution of the input pulse (the source wavelet) with the waveguide's nonlinear temporal 'reflectivity'—its hidden soliton and radiation content. By adapting seismic deconvolution algorithms, this research aims to develop an inverse computational tool that, from measured input and output pulses, extracts a clear profile of the soliton amplitudes, velocities (time delays), and dispersive wave background, providing a direct and intuitive characterization method complementary to the mathematical Inverse Scattering Transform.

ideas.title: Acoustic Metamaterial-Inspired Dispersion Engineering for Soliton Wavelength Tuning
ideas.description: This idea applies the principle of bandgap engineering from acoustic metamaterials and phononic crystals to optical dispersion engineering. In acoustics, geometric tuning of unit cells (like resonators or scatterers) is used to create and shift acoustic band gaps. Adapted to photonics, the waveguide's cross-sectional geometry (e.g., core dimensions, cladding patterning, or introduced periodic corrugations) is treated as a tunable 'photonic unit cell.' By computationally designing and fabricating waveguides with specific, spatially varying geometric parameters, one can sculpt the photonic band structure to relocate the anomalous dispersion region—where the group velocity dispersion is negative—to a chosen target wavelength. This method provides a powerful and flexible route to engineer the dispersion landscape, enabling the deterministic generation of optical solitons at wavelengths previously inaccessible with standard waveguide designs, such as moving soliton operation deeper into the mid-infrared or to specific visible wavelengths for applications in spectroscopy or sensing.

ideas.title: Spatio-Thermal Soliton Tomography via Neural Networks
ideas.description: This research direction develops a neural network framework trained on synthetic spectral data generated from a coupled nonlinear-thermal model. The model integrates the nonlinear Schrödinger equation with a spatially resolved heat diffusion equation to account for radial temperature gradients in the gain medium. The trained network can then ingest an experimental optical spectrum and output not only the soliton parameters (order, pulse width, chirp) but also a map of the active region's temperature distribution. This provides a powerful, rapid diagnostic for stabilizing mode-locked lasers and understanding thermal-nonlinear interactions in photonic systems.

ideas.title: Inverse scattering analysis of thermal soliton dynamics in dense VCSEL arrays
ideas.description: This research fuses the inverse scattering transform (IST) with scale analysis of thermal crosstalk to model the nonlinear optical pulse dynamics in silicon-based VCSEL arrays. It treats the array's combined thermal profile and refractive index distribution as a potential in a nonlinear Schrödinger equation, whose soliton content is decoded via IST. This allows the identification of stable, self-reinforcing thermal-optical soliton states that emerge from crosstalk, which traditional scale analysis alone cannot predict. The resulting framework provides a fundamental tool to design array geometries and driving conditions that suppress detrimental solitonic crosstalk modes, enhancing power scalability.

ideas.title: Nonlinearity-Enhanced Slot Waveguides via Metamaterial Cladding for Low-Energy Solitons
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of achieving solitons at femtojoule energies in nanoscale slot waveguides by drawing an analogy from seismic engineering. The analogous solution is the use of tuned mass dampers (TMDs)—auxiliary, resonant subsystems attached to buildings to absorb vibrational energy. Adapted to photonics, this translates to designing a nonlinear 'meta-cladding': a nanostructured metamaterial layer fabricated around the slot waveguide. This cladding is engineered with resonant elements and high nonlinear susceptibility, optically coupled to the evanescent field in the slot. It functions as a dedicated, efficient nonlinear responder, dramatically lowering the threshold pulse energy required for soliton formation by concentrating and enhancing the nonlinear interaction in this tailored external subsystem, rather than relying solely on the weak nonlinearity of the tightly confined nanoscale mode itself.

ideas.title: Sensitivity-Driven Dispersion Engineering for Robust Soliton Microcombs
ideas.description: This research fuses dispersion engineering with global sensitivity analysis to develop a robust, multi-objective design methodology for soliton microcombs. By applying Sobol sensitivity analysis to a coupled thermal-optical nonlinear model, the work will systematically rank how waveguide geometry parameters (e.g., width, thickness, etch) influence both the target dispersion profile and the onset current for thermal rollover. This enables the identification of Pareto-optimal designs that maximize the anomalous dispersion window for soliton tuning while simultaneously minimizing thermal sensitivity. The resulting framework moves beyond single-wavelength targeting to create devices with inherent operational robustness against power-induced thermal instability.

ideas.title: Thermally Engineered Silicon Spiral Soliton Circuits for High-Power, Long-Distance On-Chip Propagation
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the thermal packaging and junction-level modeling techniques from GaN power electronics directly into the design and fabrication of long, spiral silicon soliton waveguides. The core innovation is the co-design of the waveguide's nonlinear optical properties with embedded micro-scale thermal management structures, such as thermal vias and heat spreaders, to actively extract heat from soliton propagation hotspots. This allows for the injection of significantly higher optical powers into the chip without inducing thermal runaway or damaging the soliton state, stabilizing propagation over centimeter lengths. The result is a novel platform for on-chip, high-power nonlinear photonic processing, enabling applications like optical frequency comb generation and all-optical signal regeneration that were previously constrained by thermal limits.

ideas.title: Hybrid-Cavity Stabilized Dissipative Kerr Solitons via Electronic Feedback Control

Gain is provided by a discrete III-V SOA, isolating thermal and carrier effects from the sensitive silicon nonlinear resonator where the soliton pulse is shaped. An integrated feedback loop, comprising a high-speed photodetector and control electronics, continuously monitors the intracavity power and adjusts the SOA drive to actively suppress relaxation oscillations and power drift. This enables the robust generation and long-term stabilization of dissipative Kerr solitons in a silicon photonic chip, leveraging its nonlinearity without the burden of integrated gain-induced noise.

ideas.title: Spectral Fingerprint Analysis for Soliton Parameter Extraction via Convolutional Neural Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astrophysical spectroscopy, where convolutional neural networks are used to extract stellar parameters directly from noisy, low-resolution spectra. The analogous problem—slow, model-fitting parameter extraction from complex spectral data—is solved by training CNNs on large libraries of synthetic spectra. Adapted to nonlinear optics, the research would involve generating a comprehensive dataset of synthetic spectra corresponding to various soliton types (e.g., temporal, spatial, dissipative) with randomized parameters and noise profiles. A CNN architecture would then be trained to recognize the distinctive spectral 'fingerprint' patterns and perform a direct, non-iterative regression to extract soliton order, pulse width, peak power, and other key parameters from experimental spectral measurements, dramatically accelerating analysis compared to traditional fitting methods.

ideas.title: Aperture-Dependent Thermal Management for CW-Driven Soliton Microcombs in Silicon-VCSEL Hybrids
ideas.description: This research investigates the co-design of silicon nitride microring resonators for soliton microcomb generation with monolithically integrated VCSEL-style thermal management elements. The core novelty is using a tunable oxide aperture—analogous to a VCSEL's current confinement layer—beneath the microring to precisely control the local thermal gradient and thermal impedance. By varying the aperture diameter, we can shift the dominant thermal rollover mechanism from lateral heat spreading to vertical bottlenecking, thereby creating a tailored thermal environment that either suppresses or harnesses thermal nonlinearity to initiate and stabilize modulation instability from a continuous-wave pump. This enables the systematic exploration of how engineered thermal boundaries can replace pulsed excitation for reliable soliton formation, leading to more efficient and compact frequency comb sources.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Bayesian Neural Network for Robust Soliton Characterization
ideas.description: This system employs a hybrid architecture where a neural network processes spectral data but is penalized during training for violating the governing physical model, embedding prior scientific knowledge. A Bayesian neural network layer provides predictive uncertainty, quantifying confidence in the extracted parameters like soliton order and width. This design makes the system robust to noisy or atypical experimental data by rejecting unphysical solutions and explicitly identifying uncertain results. The final output is a set of parameter estimates with credible intervals, enabling reliable, automated analysis that researchers can trust in dynamic lab environments.

ideas.title: Blind source separation techniques applied to entangled optical loss mechanisms in semiconductor lasers
ideas.description: The conventional approach to characterizing optical loss in semiconductor lasers assumes that internal loss and carrier-density-dependent effects can be cleanly separated, typically by extrapolating threshold data to zero carrier density. This mirrors the naive assumption in audio processing that individual sound sources occupy distinct frequency bands. In reality, just as multiple speakers produce overlapping spectral content in a cocktail party setting, multiple loss mechanisms in lasers (intrinsic material absorption, free-carrier absorption, intervalence band absorption, and Auger-related reabsorption) are entangled through their shared dependence on carrier density, temperature, and photon energy. The audio community solved their analogous separation problem through blind source separation techniques—particularly Independent Component Analysis—which decompose mixed signals using statistical properties rather than assuming spectral separability. Adapting this approach, we propose collecting multi-dimensional loss measurements across systematically varied operating conditions (injection current sweeps, temperature series, multi-wavelength probes, and cavity length variations) to build an over-determined observation matrix. Non-negative matrix factorization or constrained ICA, regularized by known physical scaling laws (e.g., free-carrier absorption scales roughly as carrier density to the first power, while Auger processes scale as the cube), can then decompose the total loss into physically meaningful components without presupposing their separability. This method would reveal whether the standard linear separation assumption introduces systematic bias in reported internal loss values and could quantify the true functional form of each loss mechanism's carrier-density dependence at high injection levels relevant to modern high-power laser operation.

ideas.title: Bayesian model selection for falsifiable testing of optical loss separability in semiconductor lasers
ideas.description: This work replaces the qualitative suspicion that internal optical loss and carrier-density-dependent losses are inseparable with a rigorous statistical test. A hierarchy of loss models—from fully separable to progressively coupled—is constructed and fit to systematic measurements of differential efficiency versus cavity length and temperature at varying injection levels. Bayesian model comparison yields quantitative evidence (Bayes factors) for or against loss coupling, directly identifying the injection regimes and device geometries where the separability assumption breaks down. The framework provides both a diagnostic tool for laser designers and a physically grounded parameterization of non-separable losses that can be integrated into commercial device simulators.

ideas.title: Seismology-inspired inversion framework for extracting semiconductor material parameters from thermal rollover signatures
ideas.description: This idea draws a direct analogy between inferring hard-to-measure semiconductor laser parameters from thermal rollover data and the geophysical inverse problem of determining Earth's internal structure from surface seismic recordings. In seismology, full-waveform inversion (FWI) uses adjoint-state methods to iteratively refine a model of subsurface properties until forward-simulated waveforms match observations, recovering density, wave speeds, and attenuation profiles that are impossible to measure directly. We adapt this framework to semiconductor physics: the forward model consists of coupled drift-diffusion, heat transport, and optical rate equations describing laser operation through rollover. The measured light-current (L-I) curve shape, spectral redshift, and voltage evolution near rollover serve as the 'observed waveforms.' An adjoint system derived from these coupled equations provides efficient gradient computation of a misfit functional with respect to multiple unknown parameters—Auger recombination coefficients, internal loss, carrier leakage activation energies, and anisotropic thermal conductivities—simultaneously. Regularization strategies borrowed from geophysics (Tikhonov, total-variation, and Bayesian priors from literature bounds) address ill-posedness. The key advantage over traditional single-parameter fitting is the ability to jointly invert for many coupled parameters while rigorously quantifying trade-offs and uncertainties through the model resolution matrix, directly analogous to the checkerboard resolution tests used in seismic tomography.

ideas.title: Adapting pulsed-laser tissue ablation thermal models to capture transient rollover in burst-mode semiconductor lasers
ideas.description: Conventional semiconductor laser rollover models assume a steady-state thermal resistance linking dissipated power to junction temperature rise. This assumption breaks down for pulsed or burst-mode operation where pulse durations approach or fall below the thermal diffusion timescale of the device. An analogous challenge was solved in biomedical optics: predicting tissue damage under pulsed laser irradiation required abandoning steady-state thermal assumptions in favor of time-resolved heat diffusion solutions (Green's functions or finite-element transient solvers) combined with Arrhenius-integral cumulative damage metrics. The adapted approach applies this framework to laser diode rollover by (1) replacing the scalar thermal resistance with a time-domain impulse response (thermal impedance function) of the laser heterostructure, (2) convolving the dissipated power waveform with this impulse response to obtain the transient junction temperature profile across a pulse train, and (3) feeding the temperature trajectory into thermally activated gain suppression, carrier leakage, and non-radiative recombination rate expressions integrated over time rather than evaluated at a single steady-state temperature. This captures phenomena such as progressive thermal accumulation across burst packets, asymmetric rollover between early and late pulses in a burst, and duty-cycle-dependent shifts in the rollover knee that steady-state models cannot reproduce.

ideas.title: Transient thermal rollover as a dynamic probe for extracting temperature-dependent Auger coefficients
ideas.description: This fused approach uses deliberately controlled pulsed or burst-mode laser operation to generate transient thermal rollover curves at multiple timescales, then applies inverse modeling to extract temperature-dependent Auger recombination coefficients and other loss parameters from the time-evolving light-current characteristics. Unlike steady-state inverse methods that conflate thermal and carrier-density effects into a single equilibrium rollover point, the transient approach exploits the different time constants of heat diffusion and carrier recombination to decouple these contributions, yielding separate estimates for each loss mechanism. A physics-informed inverse solver fits a coupled time-dependent thermal-electrical model to experimentally measured transient rollover trajectories across varying pulse widths and duty cycles, dramatically improving parameter identifiability. This technique transforms what is normally an unwanted parasitic effect — transient self-heating — into a powerful characterization tool that can resolve material parameters difficult or impossible to measure by conventional steady-state methods.

ideas.title: Bayesian-calibrated transient thermal rollover model with uncertainty-aware pulsed-operation predictions
ideas.description: This work replaces the steady-state thermal assumption in laser rollover models with a time-resolved thermal solver, but critically pairs it with a Bayesian inverse calibration framework that extracts thermal and recombination parameters from sub-microsecond electro-optical measurements during pulsed operation. A hierarchical prior structure accounts for wafer-level and device-level variability in thermal diffusivity and interface conductance, preventing overfitting to single-device data. The calibrated model propagates parameter uncertainties forward into rollover predictions, providing confidence intervals that degrade gracefully in regimes where calibration data are sparse. This ensures the transient model outperforms the steady-state baseline only when the data warrant it, avoiding the failure mode of introducing unconstrained free parameters.

ideas.title: Spatially-resolved Bayesian inference for VCSEL thermal-optical parameter extraction
ideas.description: This fused approach replaces the standard single-temperature VCSEL model with a radially resolved thermal profile and employs Bayesian inference to jointly estimate the spatially varying temperature field and its coupled optoelectronic parameters (local gain, carrier density, refractive index) from measurable outputs such as spectral shifts, threshold currents, and beam profiles. By encoding physical constraints on the radial temperature distribution — such as smoothness and boundary conditions — as structured priors, the Bayesian framework regularizes the inherently ill-posed inverse problem of recovering a continuous thermal map from limited measurements, while simultaneously quantifying uncertainty at each radial position. The posterior correlations between thermal and optical parameters reveal which regions of the active area most critically govern device performance and where additional measurements would most reduce predictive uncertainty. This creates a principled design-of-experiments tool for VCSEL characterization that identifies both the dominant thermal bottlenecks and the measurement strategies needed to resolve them, going beyond what either uniform-temperature Bayesian fitting or deterministic gradient models can provide independently.

ideas.title: Constructive self-heating in cryogenic VCSELs: lessons from exothermic preheating in cryogenic rocket engine ignition
ideas.description: Cryogenic VCSELs suffer from gain-cavity wavelength detuning at very low temperatures because the quantum well gain peak and the cavity resonance shift at different rates with temperature. This is the 'dual' thermal problem: unlike room-temperature operation where self-heating degrades performance, at cryogenic temperatures a controlled amount of self-heating can re-align the gain peak with the cavity mode and dramatically improve output power and efficiency. An analogous challenge exists in cryogenic rocket propulsion, where engines burning liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen must ignite and operate reliably despite components starting at temperatures far below their optimal operating range. The aerospace solution employs thermal bootstrap sequences—pyrotechnic igniters, catalytic pre-burners, and regenerative heat recycling—to precondition engine components to a functional thermal regime before and during main-stage operation. Adapting this approach, we propose designing a VCSEL drive protocol with an engineered thermal bootstrap phase: a carefully shaped sub-threshold or pulsed current waveform is applied to generate controlled Joule heating in the active region, raising it from deep cryogenic temperatures to the narrow thermal window where gain-cavity alignment is optimal. Real-time thermal feedback (via monitoring wavelength or slope efficiency) would close the loop, analogous to how regenerative cooling channels in rocket engines continuously regulate wall temperatures. This framework reframes self-heating from a parasitic effect into a precision control knob, enabling cryogenic VCSELs to achieve performance levels unattainable at either extreme temperature. The research would involve coupled electro-thermal-optical modeling, experimental validation at 4–77 K, and development of control algorithms for autonomous thermal lock-in during VCSEL startup and steady-state operation in cryogenic photonic interconnect and quantum computing environments.

ideas.title: Lattice Green's function approach to VCSEL array thermal crosstalk from many-body phonon transport theory
ideas.description: Dense VCSEL arrays suffer from inter-element thermal crosstalk that amplifies rollover beyond single-device predictions, but full 3D thermal simulation of large arrays is computationally prohibitive. An analogous problem exists in condensed matter physics, where phonon-mediated heat transport between coupled lattice sites in crystals is elegantly handled using lattice Green's functions. In that framework, each lattice site acts as a thermal source, and the mutual thermal coupling between all sites is encoded in the Green's function matrix of the discrete Laplacian operator, enabling closed-form superposition of all pairwise interactions including many-body screening effects. Adapting this approach, we model each VCSEL in the array as a discrete thermal source on a 2D lattice and construct the array thermal impedance matrix G from the analytically known single-device thermal spreading kernel (accounting for substrate thickness, thermal conductivity, and boundary conditions). The temperature rise at each element is then T = G · P(T), where P(T) is the temperature-dependent dissipated power from the self-consistent electro-thermal model of each VCSEL. Collective thermal rollover is predicted by analyzing the eigenvalue spectrum of the coupled system: the critical current density at which the array rolls over corresponds to the condition where the dominant eigenvalue of the Jacobian matrix (dT/dI) diverges. This spectral approach reduces the N-element array problem from a costly 3D FEM simulation to an N×N matrix eigenvalue problem, enabling rapid exploration of array pitch, geometry, non-uniform biasing strategies, and thermal management designs. The framework naturally extends to irregular array layouts and heterogeneous element properties, providing a unified scaling law from single devices to kilopixel-scale VCSEL arrays.

ideas.title: Disentangling thermal conductivity nonlinearity from degradation artifacts in high-bias thermal impedance measurements via pulsed transient thermoreflectance
ideas.description: This work develops a pulsed transient thermoreflectance methodology coupled with simultaneous Raman thermometry to isolate the contribution of temperature-dependent thermal conductivity to apparent thermal impedance nonlinearity under high bias conditions. By systematically varying pulse duration from nanoseconds to milliseconds, the approach separates fast intrinsic thermal conductivity changes from slow degradation mechanisms such as electromigration and trap generation that confound steady-state measurements. A physics-based decomposition framework attributes measured impedance deviations to specific mechanisms—phonon-phonon scattering reduction, interface resistance evolution, and structural degradation—enabling quantitative bounds on each contribution. The method is validated across GaN HEMTs and SiC power devices, establishing whether constant-impedance assumptions fail primarily due to fundamental material nonlinearity or measurement-confounded degradation artifacts.

ideas.title: Transfer-learning-inspired recalibration of empirical VCSEL models across material systems
ideas.description: Extending an empirical thermal rollover model from 850 nm GaAs-based VCSELs to longer-wavelength devices (980 nm, 1550 nm) is analogous to the domain adaptation problem in machine learning, where a model trained on one data distribution must generalize to a related but distinct distribution. In ML, transfer learning addresses this by retaining the shared representational structure of a pretrained model while selectively retraining domain-specific components. Adapting this strategy, we propose a hierarchical recalibration framework: (1) decompose the existing empirical model into mechanism-specific sub-models (self-heating, carrier leakage, internal optical loss, gain saturation); (2) perform a global sensitivity analysis to classify each parameter as either material-invariant (transferable across wavelength platforms) or material-dependent (requiring recalibration); (3) for each new material system, collect a minimal targeted dataset—thermal impedance measurements, temperature-dependent L-I-V curves at a few ambient temperatures—sufficient to recalibrate only the material-dependent subset. This approach dramatically reduces the experimental burden compared to full de novo model fitting for each wavelength, while preserving the physical interpretability of the original model. The framework also yields a quantitative 'transferability score' for each mechanism, revealing which physical processes change most across material platforms—providing direct physical insight into how dominant loss and saturation mechanisms shift from GaAs to InP-based VCSELs.

ideas.title: Bayesian VCSEL Parameter Extraction Inspired by Gravitational Wave Signal Characterization
ideas.description: This work draws a direct analogy between VCSEL device characterization and gravitational wave signal analysis. In gravitational wave astronomy, the LIGO/Virgo collaboration faces a fundamentally similar challenge: extracting multiple correlated physical parameters from noisy measurement data using physics-based forward models. Their solution—Bayesian parameter estimation via nested sampling (MultiNest, dynesty) and MCMC methods implemented in LALInference—produces full posterior probability distributions over all parameters simultaneously, naturally quantifying uncertainties and correlations. We adapt this methodology to VCSEL modeling by treating rate equation models (carrier, photon, and thermal equations) as analogues to gravitational waveform templates. Noisy LIV curves, relative intensity noise spectra, and small-signal modulation response data serve as the 'detector output.' Physics-informed priors constrain parameters to physically meaningful ranges (e.g., positive recombination coefficients, bounded internal quantum efficiency). Nested sampling efficiently explores the high-dimensional parameter space while computing the Bayesian evidence, enabling rigorous model selection—for instance, discriminating between oxide-aperture-dominated and thermally-dominated current confinement models. The resulting posterior distributions reveal parameter degeneracies (such as the well-known correlation between internal loss and injection efficiency) that deterministic least-squares fitting obscures, providing VCSEL designers with calibrated confidence intervals for predictive device optimization.

ideas.title: Aperture-diameter-dependent thermal crosstalk mapping in dense VCSEL arrays
ideas.description: This work constructs a unified thermal model that simultaneously sweeps oxide aperture diameter and array pitch to map how the dominant rollover mechanism of each VCSEL element shifts under the influence of neighbor-induced thermal crosstalk. By treating aperture size as a tunable parameter across a dense array—rather than studying it in isolation or assuming identical devices—the model captures emergent collective phenomena such as spatially non-uniform rollover onset, where center elements thermally roll over via different mechanisms than edge elements depending on aperture geometry. The framework enables the design of heterogeneous-aperture arrays in which aperture diameters are intentionally graded across the array to equalize rollover thresholds and maximize aggregate power, a strategy inaccessible to either single-device aperture studies or uniform-array crosstalk analyses alone. Experimental validation is proposed using spatially resolved electroluminescence and thermal imaging of fabricated arrays with systematically varied aperture-pitch combinations.

ideas.title: Adapting ECG arrhythmia classification approaches for automated VCSEL thermal rollover mechanism identification
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between automated arrhythmia mechanism classification from ECG waveforms in cardiology and automated thermal rollover mechanism classification from L-I-V curves in VCSEL photonics. In the biomedical domain, ML models (particularly 1D-CNNs and ensemble methods) have been successfully trained to distinguish between different cardiac arrhythmia mechanisms by learning subtle morphological features in voltage-time traces recorded across large patient cohorts. The adapted approach applies the same paradigm to VCSEL characterization: L-I-V curves are treated as analogous waveform signals where the shape of the optical power rollover region encodes information about the dominant degradation mechanism. Feature extraction techniques proven in ECG analysis—such as wavelet decomposition for multi-scale shape analysis, derivative-based inflection point detection, and learned convolutional features—are adapted to capture the signatures of distinct rollover causes including carrier leakage (gradual slope reduction), thermal lensing (abrupt kink), and gain saturation (smooth compression). A labeled training set is constructed by correlating L-I-V curve shapes with mechanism assignments from physics-based simulations or expert annotation, then classifiers are deployed for high-throughput screening of VCSEL wafers to map mechanism prevalence across populations, enabling targeted process optimization.
