ideas.title: Pharmacokinetic-inspired global sensitivity analysis for thermal rollover parameter ranking
ideas.description: This idea transfers the mature sensitivity analysis pipeline used in systems pharmacology to the problem of thermal rollover in photonic or electronic devices. In pharmacokinetic modeling, complex physiological models with 30+ parameters are routinely screened using Morris elementary effects to discard insensitive parameters, followed by Sobol variance-based decomposition to quantify first-order and total-order sensitivity indices for the remaining drivers of nonlinear threshold phenomena such as drug toxicity onset. We adapt this two-stage protocol to a thermal model of rollover onset current: (1) apply Morris screening across all extracted device parameters to reduce dimensionality at low computational cost, then (2) compute Sobol indices including second-order interaction terms to rank the dominant parameters and parameter pairs driving the onset of thermal runaway. The pharmacological analogy is particularly apt because both problems involve a critical threshold (toxicity concentration vs. rollover current) governed by coupled nonlinear processes, and both benefit from distinguishing additive parameter effects from synergistic interactions. The resulting sensitivity ranking directly informs which device parameters to prioritize for manufacturing tolerance control or design optimization to maximize rollover-free operating range.

ideas.title: Neuroscience-inspired thermal-bandwidth degradation modeling: Adapting ion channel gating kinetics frameworks to VCSEL rollover analysis
ideas.description: This idea draws a structural analogy between thermally-induced high-frequency signaling failure in neurons and thermally-induced modulation bandwidth degradation in VCSELs. In computational neuroscience, the Hodgkin-Huxley framework models how temperature shifts alter ion channel gating kinetics — each channel type (Na+, K+, leak) has its own Q10 temperature coefficient governing activation/inactivation rates. As temperature rises beyond an optimal range, specific channels fail to recover quickly enough, degrading maximum firing frequency and spike shape fidelity. Bhatt et al. and others used bifurcation analysis to identify critical temperature thresholds where qualitative changes in signaling behavior occur, separating the contributions of each channel type to the overall failure. Adapting this to VCSEL physics, each thermal rollover mechanism (carrier leakage over heterobarriers, temperature-dependent gain compression, internal self-heating feedback, and Auger recombination enhancement) can be treated as an independent dynamical subsystem with its own Arrhenius or Q10-like temperature scaling. A coupled rate-equation model incorporating these modular thermal dependencies would then be subjected to bifurcation analysis to identify critical temperature thresholds where each mechanism begins to dominate bandwidth limitation. The modulation response (S21) and simulated eye diagrams would be computed across temperature, with each mechanism selectively enabled or disabled to quantify its isolated impact — directly paralleling the neuroscience technique of pharmacologically blocking individual ion channels. This approach yields a ranked hierarchy of thermal failure modes at each operating temperature, providing clear engineering targets: if carrier leakage dominates above 70°C, deeper quantum wells or electron-blocking layers are prioritized; if gain compression dominates, cavity photon lifetime tuning or p-doping optimization is indicated. The neuroscience-inspired decomposition transforms an empirically opaque rollover-bandwidth correlation into a mechanistically transparent, actionable design framework.

ideas.title: Sensitivity-guided spatially resolved thermal profiling for thermal rollover diagnostics
ideas.description: This fused approach replaces the single effective temperature assumption with a radially discretized thermal profile across the active region and then applies Sobol or Morris global sensitivity analysis to rank the influence of each spatial zone's temperature and associated material parameters on the thermal rollover onset current. By coupling spatial resolution with variance-based sensitivity decomposition, the method reveals not just that temperature gradients exist, but precisely which radial positions and thermal parameters dominate rollover behavior through nonlinear interactions invisible to one-at-a-time analyses. The resulting sensitivity maps can guide both improved parameter extraction protocols that focus measurement effort on the most influential zones and targeted thermal management strategies that address the specific spatial regions driving performance degradation. This synergy transforms spatially resolved thermal modeling from a descriptive correction into a prescriptive diagnostic tool with quantified parameter importance rankings.

ideas.title: Bayesian Multi-Observable Inference of Semiconductor Material Parameters from Thermal Rollover Under Identifiability Constraints
ideas.description: This work develops a Bayesian inference framework that extracts hard-to-measure material parameters—such as Auger recombination coefficients and carrier leakage rates—by jointly fitting thermal rollover characteristics measured across multiple operating conditions (temperature, pulse duration, cavity geometry) alongside complementary observables including spectral red-shift and efficiency droop. A Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampler explores the full posterior distribution over the parameter space, while an information-theoretic identifiability analysis determines which parameter combinations are resolvable and which remain fundamentally degenerate. By explicitly quantifying uncertainty and parameter correlations, the method avoids the false precision of deterministic single-curve inversion and provides experimentalists with actionable confidence intervals. The framework is validated on synthetic data with known ground truth before application to measured devices, establishing both accuracy and the minimum experimental dataset required for reliable extraction.

ideas.title: Spatially resolved thermal profiles for active regions via stellar atmosphere modeling techniques
ideas.description: The uniform effective temperature assumption for active regions mirrors an outdated simplification in stellar astrophysics, where entire stellar photospheres were once characterized by a single temperature. Stellar atmosphere codes like ATLAS and PHOENIX solved this by dividing the atmosphere into radial layers and solving coupled radiative transfer and energy balance equations layer by layer, producing realistic temperature stratification. Applying this analogy, we propose discretizing solar or stellar active regions into concentric radial shells — from the magnetically heated core outward to the quiet-Sun boundary — and solving for the temperature in each shell using local energy balance equations that account for magnetic heating, Spitzer thermal conduction, radiative losses, and enthalpy fluxes. The iterative solution procedure converges on a self-consistent radial temperature profile, replacing the single effective temperature with a spatially resolved thermal structure. This approach naturally captures phenomena such as hot-core/cool-periphery gradients, transition region steepening, and differential emission measure distributions that a single-temperature model cannot reproduce, leading to more accurate predictions of EUV/X-ray emission, coronal loop diagnostics, and energy budget estimates for active regions.

ideas.title: Physics-informed multi-modal deep learning for disambiguating VCSEL thermal rollover mechanisms
ideas.description: A physics-informed multi-modal classification framework that fuses L-I-V curves with spectral shift trajectories, impedance response, and near-field emission profiles to disambiguate degenerate thermal rollover mechanisms in VCSELs. The model architecture encodes known thermal and carrier transport equations as differentiable constraints, breaking the degeneracy inherent in single-curve classification while providing calibrated uncertainty estimates for each mechanism attribution. A synthetic data augmentation pipeline leveraging electro-thermal VCSEL simulation generates physically consistent training examples across mechanism mixtures, addressing the chronic scarcity of labeled experimental rollover data. This approach transforms rollover classification from a pattern-matching exercise into a physics-grounded diagnostic tool capable of identifying co-occurring mechanisms and flagging ambiguous cases for expert review.

ideas.title: Sensitivity-ranked redesign of VCSEL epitaxial layers inspired by turbine blade topology optimization
ideas.description: Gas turbine blade designers face a structurally analogous problem: a multilayer system (substrate, bond coat, thermal barrier coating, cooling channels) where thermal failure is governed by a ranked set of contributors, and redesign budgets are limited. They solve this by performing adjoint sensitivity analysis to rank each layer's and geometric feature's contribution to peak temperature, then applying topology optimization preferentially to the top-ranked contributors. Adapting this to VCSEL thermal rollover, we propose: (1) perform a full adjoint thermal-electrical sensitivity analysis on the VCSEL epitaxial stack to quantitatively rank each layer's contribution to junction temperature rise at rollover; (2) formulate a constrained multi-objective optimization targeting the top-ranked layers — varying DBR mirror pair composition and period, oxide aperture geometry, and active region quantum well barrier thickness — subject to optical and electrical performance constraints; (3) iterate using surrogate-model-assisted optimization (Bayesian optimization or Kriging), analogous to the surrogate models used in turbine blade design, to efficiently navigate the high-dimensional epitaxial design space. This approach replaces ad hoc trial-and-error epitaxial redesign with a principled, sensitivity-guided optimization framework that allocates design effort where it matters most for thermal rollover suppression.

ideas.title: ML-driven inverse extraction of material parameters from classified VCSEL rollover signatures
ideas.description: Train ML classifiers on L-I-V curves to first identify the dominant thermal rollover mechanism (e.g., Auger recombination, carrier leakage, gain-temperature sensitivity) for each VCSEL in a population, then use the classified mechanism label to select a mechanism-specific inverse model that extracts the corresponding hard-to-measure material parameters from the rollover characteristics. By decomposing the inverse problem into a classification step followed by a constrained parameter extraction step, the approach avoids the ill-posedness of trying to simultaneously fit all possible material parameters under an unknown mechanism, dramatically improving identifiability and accuracy. The large-scale population data enables statistical validation of extracted parameters across devices sharing the same epitaxial structure, providing confidence bounds on inferred quantities like Auger coefficients, thermal resistance contributions, and carrier leakage activation energies that would be inaccessible from single-device measurements alone.

ideas.title: Spatially resolved thermal co-design: power-electronics packaging guided by radial VCSEL temperature maps
ideas.description: This fused idea replaces the conventional uniform active-region temperature assumption with finite-element radial thermal profiles and then uses those spatially resolved maps to drive the design of heterogeneous packaging solutions borrowed from GaN power transistor thermal management. Rather than applying a blanket heat-spreading layer, the approach co-optimizes micro-scale thermal structures — such as spatially graded diamond or SiC interlayers, variable-pitch micro-channel coolers, and patterned metallic vias — whose geometry is directly informed by the predicted hot-spot distribution across the VCSEL mesa. This co-design loop feeds updated thermal boundary conditions back into the radial temperature model, iterating until the active-region gradient falls below a target uniformity threshold. The result is a VCSEL thermal management strategy that simultaneously reduces peak junction temperature and flattens the radial thermal profile, improving both threshold current stability and beam quality at high drive currents.

ideas.title: Adapting strain-dependent permeability models from geomechanics to capture power-dependent thermal impedance in electronic devices
ideas.description: In geomechanics and reservoir engineering, the assumption of constant permeability was abandoned decades ago after field observations showed that rock permeability changes nonlinearly with stress and temperature. Engineers developed coupled thermo-hydro-mechanical (THM) simulators that iterate between mechanical deformation, thermal transport, and fluid flow until self-consistency is reached, using stress-dependent permeability relations such as Kozeny-Carman or exponential compaction models. This research adapts that same iterative self-consistent coupling strategy to the thermal management of high-power electronic devices. Just as permeability depends on the stress state it helps determine, thermal impedance in semiconductors depends on the temperature field it governs—thermal conductivity of silicon decreases roughly as T^(-1.3) and GaN as T^(-1.4), creating a nonlinear feedback loop at high bias. The proposed approach replaces the standard constant-thermal-impedance compact model with a power-dependent thermal impedance function derived from self-consistent electro-thermal simulation, analogous to THM coupling. Specifically, the device thermal network would be solved iteratively: (1) compute temperature distribution assuming current thermal conductivity values, (2) update thermal conductivity at each node based on the new temperature, (3) repeat until convergence. The resulting nonlinear thermal impedance curves would be parameterized as functions of dissipated power and ambient temperature, then validated against infrared thermography measurements on GaN HEMTs and SiC MOSFETs under pulsed and DC high-bias conditions. This framework would identify critical power thresholds beyond which the constant-impedance approximation introduces unacceptable errors in junction temperature prediction, directly impacting reliability modeling and safe operating area definitions.

ideas.title: Model-ensemble robust sensitivity analysis for thermal rollover onset parameters
ideas.description: This approach performs Sobol sensitivity analysis across an ensemble of competing surrogate models—physics-based compact thermal models, polynomial chaos expansions, and Gaussian process emulators—each mapping extracted device parameters to thermal rollover onset current. By comparing sensitivity indices across structurally distinct surrogates, the method distinguishes genuinely influential parameters from those whose apparent importance is an artifact of model misspecification. Parameters consistently ranked as dominant across all surrogates are identified as robust drivers of rollover onset, while discordant rankings trigger model refinement or additional experimental characterization. This ensemble-robust framework guards against the critical failure mode of drawing erroneous physical conclusions from sensitivity analysis performed on a single, potentially misspecified model.

ideas.title: Physics-constrained radial thermal parameterization for active region emission modeling
ideas.description: Replace the uniform active region temperature assumption with a radial thermal profile governed by a physics-informed parameterization rooted in radiative-conductive energy transport equations. Instead of freely fitting temperature at each spatial element, the profile is described by 2-3 physically motivated parameters—peak temperature, characteristic heating scale length, and a boundary flux condition—that encode thermal transport constraints and break the degeneracies inherent in unconstrained spatially resolved models. This approach captures meaningful temperature gradients while maintaining identifiability from spectroscopic or imaging observations, and enables robust Bayesian inference with informative physical priors. The framework is validated by demonstrating improved spectral fits over uniform models without the overfitting risks of free-form thermal maps.

ideas.title: Identifiability-Aware Bayesian Inference for VCSEL Parameters via Physics-Informed Reparameterization and Optimal Experimental Design
ideas.description: This work addresses the fundamental identifiability crisis in Bayesian VCSEL parameter estimation by first performing a structural identifiability analysis of the coupled rate equations to expose inherent parameter degeneracies. It introduces a physics-informed reparameterization that maps correlated physical parameters onto identifiable combinations (dimensionless groups and characteristic ratios), transforming pathological posterior geometries into well-conditioned distributions amenable to efficient sampling. A normalizing-flow variational inference backend captures residual non-Gaussian structure while scaling to high-dimensional parameter spaces. Finally, a Bayesian optimal experimental design loop prescribes which measurements (modulation frequencies, bias currents, temperatures) to acquire next in order to maximally resolve remaining parameter uncertainties, closing the loop between inference and experiment.
